sectdars__001_a.text:
Proposed training and development programme
Training in skills necessary to support the proposed research
My NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and Clinical Lectureship have provided me with a strong foundation in big data
analysis, epidemiology and implementation science. An Advanced Fellowship would give me the opportunity to
develop new skills that will be incredibly helpful for this research proposal and in my future career as an
independent researcher in data-driven quality improvement:
 In Years 1 and 2, I will complete a part-time MSc in Health Data Science at the London School of
Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. This course aligns closely with the skills I will be using in Workstreams 1
and 3 of my proposal. It will be a fantastic opportunity to advance my skills as a health data scientist,
including modules dedicated to machine learning, predictive modelling and Bayesian analysis.
 In Year 2, I will complete the Developing and Evaluating Complex Interventions course at the
National Centre for Research Methods, University of Southampton. This course is led by Dr Kathyrn
Skivington, lead author of the MRC/NIHR Complex Intervention Development Framework, which I will be
using to develo
sectdars__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_b.text:
p a complex intervention in Workstream 2 of my proposal. The skills I learn in this course
will complement the training I received in Health Behaviour Change (University of Oxford) and
Implementation Science (King’s College London) during my Doctoral Fellowship.
 In Year 3, I will complete the Design and Analysis of Cluster Randomised and Stepped Wedge Trials
course at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. This course is led by Professor John
Bradley, an expert in cluster randomised trials. It covers many of the skills I will be utilising in Workstream
3 of my proposal, including cluster randomisation procedures, participant- and cluster-level analysis of
treatment effects, and ethical considerations in cluster randomised trials.
These courses will complement the training and expertise available to me at King’s College London, including
from experts in epidemiology (Professor James Galloway), data-enabled trial design (Professor Martin Gulliford),
experimental medicine (Professor Andrew Cope), statistics (Professor Sam Norton), implementation science
(Centre for Implementation Science), and PPIE (Dr Nicky Wilson).
Placements with other groups
During Year 1 of my Fell
sectdars__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_c.text:
owship, I will complete a 3-month placement (1 day a week) with software developers
at the Bennett Institute for Applied Data Science, University of Oxford. This will give me the opportunity to
learn one-on-one from the team of professional coders, database engineers and data visualisation experts behind
the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment (led by Professor Ben Goldacre), which I will be utilising in
Workstreams 1 and 3.
Throughout my Fellowship, I will be working closely with the Data Analytics and Implementation Support teams
at NICE to develop the infrastructure needed to monitor the real-world uptake of clinical guidelines. This will be an
incredible learning and career development opportunity. It will enable me to influence the development and
implementation of national guidelines across numerous disease areas.
Training to support my development as a future health research leader
I will apply to the Emerging Research Leaders stream of NIHR’s Future-Focused Leadership programme,
which is designed specifically for post-doctoral researchers taking up their first significant leadership and
management roles. Through a mixture of residential workshops and training modules, I
sectdars__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_d.text:
will be able to explore the
management and leadership skills that are essential for a successful career in health research, including the ability
to inspire other team members, build strong collaborations with external organisations, and the importance of
strategic awareness.
To further my skills in PPIE, I will complete a series of workshops at the National Coordinating Centre for Public
Engagement, including modules in community engagement and partnership working, in addition to the dedicated
PPIE training sessions at the NIHR BRC, University College London Hospitals.
I will take full advantage of the training opportunities available to health researchers at King’s College London,
including training programmes in Equality, Diversity and Inclusion, media engagement, and supervisory
skills.
Overseas research visit
In Year 4 of my Fellowship, I will complete a two-week placement at the University of Tsukuba, Japan, with
Professor Masao Iwagami and his team. This will give me the opportunity to learn about data-driven quality
improvement from clinical epidemiologists in an entirely different healthcare system, in a country that is renowned
for its approach to continuous quality impr
sectdars__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_e.text:
ovement. I will apply this learning to my research proposal, and share it
with my collaborators at NICE, to build an understanding of how the UK can be an exemplar for data-driven quality
improvement.
Conferences
In each year of my Fellowship, I will attend at least one national conference to present my research findings and
learn from experts in the fields of rheumatology (British Society for Rheumatology Annual Conference), primary
care (Royal College of General Practitioners Annual Conference) and quality improvement (NHS Providers
Quality & Improvement Conference). I have also costed in attendance at two international conferences during my
Fellowship, to give me the opportunity to engage with leading health researchers from Europe and the United
States.
Planned clinical sessions
I am requesting 20% of my time to support two clinical sessions as a consultant rheumatologist. I will undertake a
weekly clinic in inflammatory arthritis at King’s College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust, and establish a monthly,
multi-disciplinary, crystal arthritis clinic in collaboration with my colleagues at Guy’s and St Thomas’ Hospital
NHS Foundation Trust. This clinic will provide best practice ca
sectdars__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_f.text:
re for people with gout and other crystal
arthropathies, which aligns closely with the aims of my Fellowship. There is a huge clinical need for this service,
with very few dedicated clinics in the UK for people with severe gout (e.g. those with end-stage renal disease,
severe tophaceous gout or recurrent hospitalisations). It will be fantastic opportunity to establish and lead such a
service.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1
Name Professor James Galloway
Institution King's College London
Position Professor of Rheumatology
Description of support provided
James Galloway is a Professor of Rheumatology at King’s College London and Honorary Consultant
Rheumatologist at King’s College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust. He is the lead methodologist for the National
Early Inflammatory Arthritis Audit in England and Wales; methodology advisor to the British Society for
Rheumatology; and Deputy Director of the King’s College London Clinical Research Facility. Professor Galloway’s
expertise in epidemiology, quality improvement and clinical trials, make him ideally placed to support my research
proposal and career development.
Professor Galloway has been a fantastic mentor to me throughout my clini
sectdars__001_f.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_g.text:
cal academic career. He was my
primary supervisor during my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, and is the academic supervisor for my NIHR Clinical
Lectureship. He has been incredibly supportive of me in developing my own research niche in data-driven quality
improvement, and in offering me his time and expertise as I developed this Advanced Fellowship proposal.
Professor Galloway is ideally placed to support my development into an independent health researcher. He has
extensive experience as a supervisor and early career mentor, and he is Programme Director for Integrated
Academic Training at King’s College London. He has several senior management roles within higher education,
and collaborates closely with national organisations including NHS England, CQC and HQIP, to whom my
research is closely aligned. Importantly, Professor Galloway is familiar with the challenges of navigating clinical
work as an NHS rheumatologist, alongside a successful academic career.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 2
Name Associate Professor Laura Coates
Institution University of Oxford
Position NIHR Clinician Scientist
Description of support provided
Laura Coates is an associate professor and previous NIHR Clinicia
sectdars__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_h.text:
n Scientist at the University of Oxford, and
honorary consultant rheumatologist at Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust. She is based outside of
my institution and will be less directly involved in the day-to-day running of my project, with a greater focus on my
career development and progression.
Professor Coates is a fantastic role model for how to build a successful career as a health researcher. She has
extensive experience of delivering successful grants, including a £1.8 million NIHR EME grant exploring precision
medicine in psoriatic arthritis, and a £2.3 million NIHR HSDR grant evaluating whether patient-initiated care leads
to improved outcomes in rheumatology. She is a co-lead on international consortia grants totalling over €30
million. She has a strong background in clinical trials and translational medicine, and has led several national and
international clinical guidelines. She is a leading clinical academic in the management of psoriatic arthritis – an
inflammatory joint condition that, like gout, is associated with multiple long-term health conditions (including
metabolic syndrome) and adverse health outcomes if undertreated. During her NIHR Fellowship,
sectdars__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_i.text:
she established
an NHS service that delivers optimal (‘treat-to-target’) management for patients with psoriatic arthritis; something I
hope to replicate for patients with gout during my Fellowship.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 3
Name Professor Edward Roddy
Institution Keele University
Position Professor of Rheumatology
Description of support provided
Edward Roddy is a Professor of Rheumatology at Keele University and Honorary Consultant Rheumatologist at
the Haywood Hospital, Midlands Partnership University NHS Foundation Trust. He is Chair of the British Society
for Rheumatology (BSR) Guidelines Steering Group.
Professor Roddy is a leading expert in the management of gout. He has led numerous, highly successful, research
grants in this field, spanning epidemiology and clinical trials, including grants funded by the NIHR Health
Technology Assessment, NIHR Research for Patient Benefit Programme, and NIHR School for Primary Care
Research. He led the 2017 BSR Gout Guideline and was clinical topic advisor to the 2022 NICE Gout Guideline.
He therefore has critical insight into the guideline I will be using to develop a data-driven approach to monitoring
guideline uptake.
Professor Rodd
sectdars__001_i.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_j.text:
y’s support and guidance was a key driver behind the success of my doctoral research. He has
also provided external mentorship to other academic clinicians, resulting in successful NIHR doctoral and post-
doctoral fellowship applications. His broad methodological expertise and experience in delivering successful
research grants in the field of crystal arthritis make him an ideal mentor for me. His ongoing support will help to
ensure the success of my fellowship proposal and my development into a leading clinical academic in the field of
crystal arthritis.
Collaborations
I have established a strong collaboration with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) to
support my research proposal and career development. In Workstream 1, I will be collaborating closely with the
Data Analytics team at NICE (led by Shaun Rowark) to develop a data-driven framework for monitoring the uptake
and impact of national guidelines. In Workstreams 2 and 3, I will work with the Implementation Support team at
NICE to develop interventions that bridge identified evidence-practice gaps, as well as helping to target
implementation-support activities.
I am collaborating with the Bennett In
sectdars__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_k.text:
stitute for Applied Data Science, University of Oxford (led by Professor
Ben Goldacre), who run the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment I will be utilising in Workstreams 1 and
3 of my proposal. OpenSAFELY is the largest and most secure health data platform in the UK. As well as
supporting the delivery of my research proposal, I will complete a 3-month training placement with the Bennett
Institute, to learn from their team of software engineers and health data experts.
I am collaborating with Clinical Effectiveness South East London and Dr Rachna Chowla (General Practitioner
and Joint Director of Clinical Strategy, King’s Health Partners), who have extensive experience of implementing
best practice care for long-term conditions in primary care. Their experience of combining education and digital
solutions (e.g. decision-support tools via EMIS/Ardens) is very relevant to the intervention I will be developing and
implementing in Workstreams 2 and 3. Additionally, I will be collaborating with Dr Shalini Ahuja (Lecturer in
Implementation Science, King’s College London), Dr Joanna Hudson (Lecturer and Health Psychologist, King’s
College London) and the Centre for Implementation Scie
sectdars__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_l.text:
nce (King’s College London) to ensure my intervention
and implementation strategies have a robust theoretical basis for enacting behaviour change.
I will be collaborating with Professor Abhishek Abhishek (Professor of Rheumatology, University of Nottingham)
and Professor Edward Roddy (Professor of Rheumatology, Keele University) who have extensive experience of
conducting trials to optimise gout management in primary care. I have engaged the support of Professor Martin
Gulliford (Professor of Public Health and Epidemiology, King’s College London), who has pioneered the use of
data-enabled, cluster-randomised trials in primary care settings. Their combined expertise and support will be
invaluable for the data-enabled, primary care-based trial I undertake in Workstream 3.
As well as my patient partners, I will be collaborating with the UK Gout Society throughout my Fellowship. The
UK Gout Society are the leading patient organisation for people living with gout in the UK. Their input will help
ensure that my research benefits as many people as possible with this highly treatable, yet poorly managed,
condition. I have also engaged the support of Dr Benjamin Ellis MBE, senior policy adv
sectdars__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_m.text:
isor to Versus
Arthritis, and consultant rheumatologist at Imperial College Healthcare NHS Trust. Dr Ellis’s experience in driving
improvements in healthcare for people with long-term conditions, particularly under-served groups, is highly
relevant to the aims of my Fellowship.
During my Fellowship, I will complete a visiting research placement with Professor Masao Iwagami at the
University of Tsukuba, Japan. This will enable me to build a collaboration with a team of clinical epidemiologists
and quality improvement experts in a different healthcare system, and gain first-hand experience of how real-world
data can drive improvements in patient care.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support must be completed by the head of your lead academic department. In order to
contribute to this application, this individual must be invited to do so via the 'Participants and Signatories' section of
the form.
Statement of support
We are delighted to support this exciting application, and to host Dr Mark Russell at the Centre for Rheumatic
Diseases at King’s College London.
Why I support Dr Russell
Mark is an excellent candidate for this NIHR Advanced Fellowship. Thro
sectdars__001_m.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_n.text:
ughout his doctoral and post-doctoral
research, Mark has demonstrated incredible drive and motivation to learn new skills and collaborate. He is
conscientious, hard-working, and strives for the best, both in his academic work and for his patients. Mark’s
research outputs are testament to his dedication, having published 28 manuscripts in high-impact journals during
his 3-year NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, including first-author publications in The Lancet Rheumatology, The Lancet
Regional Health, and Annals of Rheumatic Diseases. He has been awarded national prizes, and was interviewed
by the BBC, The Times, and the Guardian on the impact of his research for people with gout.
Since joining our department in 2017, Mark has demonstrated outstanding initiative and independence for an
early-career researcher. He has formed strong collaborations with organisations including NICE, the Healthcare
Quality Improvement Partnership, and NHS England, and has developed an exciting research niche in data-driven
quality improvement. He has taken full advantage of the opportunities at King’s, including training in statistical
methodology, implementation science, and leadership and management. He has ta
sectdars__001_n.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_o.text:
ken on teaching and
examination roles for our MBBS and Integrated Academic Training programmes, and he now plays a key role in
using large-language models to develop MBBS examination questions.
Mark’s recognition of the sub-optimal care offered to patients with gout has inspired him to use this condition to
develop the methodology in this Fellowship proposal. Gout is a disease that impacts enormously on patients, for
which there is a desperate need for better care, and for which Mark has a strong track-record in delivering
successful research. Mark was awarded the prestigious British Society for Rheumatology Young Investigator
Award for his demonstration of a large increase in hospitalisations for gout in England. He collaborated extensively
with patients and other stakeholders to implement a pathway that optimised care for people hospitalised for gout.
His research has directly benefitted patients, received national recognition, and has been adopted by other NHS
hospitals.
Mark’s Advanced Fellowship proposal has the potential to transform how we monitor the quality of care that is
delivered to patients. He will deliver infrastructure that does not exist currently: the ability to e
sectdars__001_o.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_p.text:
valuate care quality in
near real-time. This is essential to improving patient outcomes and to organisations including NICE. Mark’s unique
skills in big data analysis and quality improvement methodology, combined with his clinical expertise, make him an
ideal person to lead this work.
Why King’s is the ideal host institution for Mark
The Centre for Rheumatic Diseases is a EULAR Centre of Research Excellence for rheumatology research. It has
been a launchpad for success for multiple clinical academics in the field of rheumatology and health services
research. Underlying the success of our department is cross-disciplinary leadership that bridges epidemiology,
clinical trials and qualitative methodology, integrated within NHS clinical practice. We have extremely supportive
team members, and an ethos of mentoring post-doctoral researchers through the next stage of their careers. We
prioritise training and career development over the quantity of research outputs, and we believe in the importance
of building a future generation of collaborative researchers who put patients at the centre of science.
Mark will benefit from our extensive experience of delivering high-quality health services
sectdars__001_p.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_q.text:
research using routinely-
collected healthcare data. He will receive training and methodological support from experts in clinical epidemiology
(Professor James Galloway), statistical methodology (Professor Sam Norton), experimental medicine (Professor
Andrew Cope), health psychology (Dr Joanna Hudson), and patient and public involvement (Dr Nicky Wilson). He
will have a wealth of expertise and training opportunities available in complex intervention development via the
Centre for Implementation Science at King’s, as well the support of a leading expert in data-enabled clinical trials
(Professor Martin Gulliford).
Mark will have access to a broad range of training in research methods, career development and mentorship
opportunities at King’s. Recognising that diversity of experience is essential at this stage of training, we actively
encourage training placements with other institutions and industry. Our department’s strong links with
organisations including NHS England, the Healthcare Quality Improvement Partnership, and the British Society for
Rheumatology, will provide Mark with the opportunity to become a leading collaborator in data-driven quality
improvement.
We have a strong
sectdars__001_q.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_r.text:
track record in delivering bridging support to clinical researchers on completing fellowships. We
supported Mark in obtaining funding to bridge his transition from Doctoral Fellowship to Clinical Lecturer, and as a
co-applicant on NIHR HSDR and MRC programme grants. We are committed to supporting Mark during his
Advanced Fellowship, including through the provision of PhD students and research support. King’s also has a
Fellow-to-Faculty process which he would be eligible to pursue.
We are dedicated to ensuring equality of opportunity, recognising and celebrating our diversity and inclusion. We
have been a member of the Athena SWAN Gender Equality Charter since 2007, and King’s gained its most recent
Silver institutional award in 2021. We hold a Bronze Race Equality Charter Mark Award, and we are members of
the Stonewall Diversity Champions programme, Business Disability Forum and Working Families. We have a
variety of staff networks that support our equality, diversity, and inclusion strategy, including NEST (parents and
carers), Race Equality, Access King’s (disability equality), Elevate (gender equality), and Proudly King’s
(LGBTQIA+ equality). We are holders of the European Com
sectdars__001_r.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_s.text:
mission's HR Excellence in Research Award, in
recognition of our commitment to the principles of the Concordat to Support the Career Development for
Researchers.
I strongly believe that Mark is an excellent candidate for this NIHR Advanced Fellowship. It will provide him with
dedicated training and expertise in health data analysis and implementation science, and enable him to establish
himself as an independent researcher in this field. We offer our unreserved support to Mark, and we are committed
to ensuring that the infrastructure, training and research support are in place for him to succeed.
sectdars__001_s.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

implications: Applicant CV: CV details extensive research experience including NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, postdoctoral roles, and specific methodologies like big data analysis and implementation science, strongly supporting relevant re
Applicant Research Background: Details extensive experience in big data analysis, implementation science, and RCTs, with formal training in epidemiology and statistics, strongly supporting g.3.

Training & Development and Research Support: Details prior training in big data, epidemiology, and implementation science, and outlines specific upcoming courses (MSc Health Data Science, Complex Interventions) directly

Applicant CV: CV lists numerous high-impact publications in journals like The Lancet Rheumatology and Rheumatology, plus multiple awards (e.g., Eric Bywaters Prize), demonstrating strong research outputs and recognition.
Applicant Research Background: Lists 28 publications including 4 first-author in The Lancet, multiple awards, and a guideline cited as the most-cited manuscript in Rheumatology 2023, supporting g.4.

Training & Development and Research Support: Host statement highlights 28 high-impact publications (Lancet, Annals of Rheumatic Diseases) and awards (BSR Young Investigator Award), confirming strong research outputs.

Applicant CV: Progression from trainee to Principal Investigator on grants (PREVENT Study) and Lead Analyst on industry grants shows increasing responsibility and trajectory towards research leadership.
Applicant Research Background: Demonstrates leadership through managing teams, leading national guideline development, and supervising fellows/PhD students, supporting g.5.

Training & Development and Research Support: Applicant plans to join NIHR Future-Focused Leadership programme and has taken on teaching/examination roles, demonstrating trajectory towards research leadership.

Applicant CV: CV shows dual career path with clinical roles (Rheumatology Specialist Trainee, NIHR Clinical Lecturer) alongside research positions, confirming commitment to a practitioner-academic career.
Applicant Research Background: Confirms dual role as Rheumatologist and NIHR Clinical Lecturer with clinical teaching and research supervision, supporting g.6.

Training & Development and Research Support: Applicant requests 20% time for clinical sessions as a consultant rheumatologist and plans to establish a new clinic, confirming commitment to a practitioner-academic career.
Applicant CV: CV provides concrete evidence of prior successful projects (TaILOR, PREVENT) and a robust publication record, directly supporting the quality of previous research.
Applicant Research Background: Clearly articulates a vision to become a leader in data-driven quality improvement and secure a tenured position, supporting g.1.

Training & Development and Research Support: Clearly articulates a vision to become an independent researcher in data-driven quality improvement, supported by a detailed training plan and leadership development.
Applicant Research Background: Explicitly states the award is the 'best possible opportunity' to achieve career goals and secure tenure, directly addressing g.2.

Training & Development and Research Support: Explicitly states the Advanced Fellowship is the opportunity to develop new skills (MSc, complex interventions) essential for the proposal and future career as an independent
Applicant Research Background: Describes fundamental patient involvement in research design via interviews, workshops, and PPIE events, supporting wp.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Confirms research plan shaped by patient interviews and ongoing collaboration with patient partners, supporting wp.1.
Scientific Abstract: Describes using focus groups to identify barriers and PPIE events for dissemination, supporting integration of lived experience.

Detailed Research Plan: Details co-design of the intervention through focus groups with patients and professionals, and use of a patient advisory group to develop topic guides, strongly supporting wp.1.
Patient & Public Involvement: Details co-design via interviews and stakeholder meetings that shifted focus to community care, with public partners shaping study design and intervention ideas.

Training & Development and Research Support: Applicant plans PPIE workshops and collaborates with the UK Gout Society to ensure research benefits patients, reinforcing integration of lived experience.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms a dedicated 'Patient & Public Involvement' section with 766 words, supporting the presence of PPI content for wp.1.
Applicant Research Background: Highlights work with patient organizations and media engagement to ensure research benefits patients, supporting wp.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Outlines dissemination via conferences, papers, and working with communities to maximize benefit, supporting wp.3.
Scientific Abstract: Outlines plans to share code openly and communicate via conferences and social media to maximize public and patient benefit.

Detailed Research Plan: Outlines a comprehensive dissemination plan including plain language summaries, social media, and engagement with patient organizations to maximize public benefit, supporting wp.3.
Patient & Public Involvement: Outlines comprehensive dissemination plans including plain language summaries, social media, and patient-led presentations to maximize public benefit.

Training & Development and Research Support: Collaboration with NICE and patient organizations aims to influence national guidelines and maximize benefit for people with gout, supporting public and patient benefit.
Applicant Research Background: Identifies under-served groups (ethnic minorities, older adults) as a focus of recent research, supporting wp.4.
Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly targets under-served groups (ethnic minorities, socioeconomically disadvantaged) in focus group recruitment and analysis stratification, supporting wp.4.
Patient & Public Involvement: Explicitly identifies under-served groups (multiple long-term conditions) and plans broad representation in the advisory group to ensure their inclusion.
Plain English Summary of Research: Clearly articulates problem, aims, methods, beneficiaries, and impact in accessible language, strongly supporting pr.1.
Plain English NLP Analysis: NLP metrics show Flesch-Kincaid grade 7.73, 0% long sentences, and coverage of problem/objectives/methods/beneficiaries/impact, strongly supporting pr.1.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Plain English Summary and NLP analysis sections, supporting structural completeness for pr.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Defines specific tasks (checking guidelines, making a plan, testing in 38 practices over 12 months), indicating feasible scope for pr.3.
Scientific Abstract: Defines a 60-month timeline with three distinct workstreams and specific sample sizes (n=38 practices), supporting feasibility and scope appropriateness.

Detailed Research Plan: Defines a 60-month timeline with three distinct workstreams, specific sample sizes (38 practices, 1.45m patients), and power calculations, confirming feasibility and scope appropriateness for pr.3
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes using OpenSAFELY data for 58 million people and a comparative study in 38 practices, supporting pr.4.
Scientific Abstract: Details specific methods including regression modelling, cluster-randomised trials, and use of OpenSAFELY data, demonstrating methodological rigor.

Detailed Research Plan: Provides explicit methodological details including interrupted time-series, cluster-randomised trial design, minimisation, and handling of missing data, demonstrating high methodological rigor for
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly states benefits including reduced pain, better care, and NHS cost reduction, strongly supporting pr.5.
Scientific Abstract: Explicitly lists patient benefits (reduced pain, disability) and system benefits (reduced hospitalisations, healthcare inequality), supporting impact criteria.

Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly outlines benefits for patients (reduced pain, complications), clinicians (reduced workload), and the NHS (cost savings, reduced inequality), strongly supporting pr.5.
Plain English Summary of Research: Details PPI involvement in shaping the plan, creating resources, and reviewing findings with patient partners, supporting pr.6.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions convening focus groups with stakeholders and communicating findings through PPIE events, indicating engagement with people and communities.
Detailed Research Plan: Details extensive engagement via focus groups with patients and professionals, a patient advisory group, and a nested qualitative study, strongly supporting pr.6.

Detailed Research Plan: Critically reviews limitations of manual audits and existing data platforms (CPRD), identifies specific knowledge gaps in gout care implementation, and justifies the use of OpenSAFELY, strongly su

Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly plans to stratify analysis by ethnicity, deprivation, and comorbidity to address healthcare inequality, and purposively samples under-served groups for focus groups, supporting pr.9.

Detailed Research Plan: Lists specific training activities including an MSc in Health Data Science, courses on complex interventions and cluster trials, and a research visit, demonstrating a complete and relevant trainin

Training & Development and Research Support: Provides a comprehensive, structured training plan including an MSc, specific courses on complex interventions and cluster trials, and placements, directly addressing project
Application Form Analysis: Confirms a dedicated 'Training & Development' section with 3370 words, supporting the presence of a detailed training plan for td.1.
Detailed Research Plan: Maps training directly to project needs (computational analysis for Workstream 1, intervention development for Workstream 2, trial design for Workstream 3), supporting td.2.

Training & Development and Research Support: Explicitly maps training activities to specific workstreams (e.g., MSc for Workstreams 1 & 3, Complex Interventions course for Workstream 2), ensuring suitability to project n
Detailed Research Plan: Links training to career goals of becoming an independent researcher and securing tenure via the Fellow-to-Faculty scheme, supporting td.3.

Training & Development and Research Support: Links training to career aspirations of becoming an independent researcher and securing tenure, with leadership training and international placements enhancing transferability
Detailed Research Plan: Describes a structured mentorship framework with bi-monthly meetings with specific mentors (Professors Galloway, Roddy, Coates) and a project steering group, supporting ss.2.

Training & Development and Research Support: Details three supervisors with specific expertise (epidemiology, clinical trials, gout management) and describes their roles in mentoring and career development.

Detailed Research Plan: Highlights access to OpenSAFELY (covering >99% of England's population) and collaboration with NICE and NHS England, demonstrating appropriate institutional support and resources for ss.3.

Training & Development and Research Support: Host provides access to experts in epidemiology, statistics, and implementation science, plus infrastructure for PhD students and a Fellow-to-Faculty process.

Detailed Research Plan: Integrates inclusivity across the lifecycle from co-design (focus groups) to analysis (stratification by deprivation) and dissemination (plain language summaries), supporting wp.5.
Patient & Public Involvement: Describes involvement across the lifecycle from proposal development and ethics approval to interpreting results and dissemination planning.

Detailed Research Plan: Mentions costing a post-doctoral research assistant for qualitative work to support inclusion and engagement, indicating resourced plans for inclusion activities, supporting wp.6.
Patient & Public Involvement: Confirms budgeted reimbursement for patient involvement and training for facilitators, demonstrating a costed and resourced plan.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Details specific budget lines for patient advisory groups, facilitator training, and public engagement events, confirming a costed and resourced plan for research inclusion (wp.6).

Training & Development and Research Support: Host institution is a EULAR Centre of Research Excellence with a track record of supporting clinical academics and delivering high-quality health services research.

Training & Development and Research Support: Host institution holds Athena SWAN Silver, Race Equality Charter Bronze, and Stonewall Diversity Champions status, demonstrating a strong commitment to inclusive culture.

SUMMARY BUDGET: Provides explicit justifications linking staff costs to specific workstreams (e.g., qualitative expertise for Workstream 2/3, statisticians for trial duration) and equipment to data analysis needs, strong
Application Form Analysis: Identifies 148 table-like budget lines and links between objectives, methods, and budget terms, supporting pr.7.

sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision to become an independent leader in data-driven quality improvement (g.1.a) and a distinctive direction combining big data with implementation science (g.1.b). The training plan is exceptionally well-justified with specific courses mapped to workstreams (g.1.c), and strong mentorship is evidenced by three named supervisors with complementary expertise and a structured meeting framework (g.1.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive' nature of the ideas relies heavily on the applicant's existing track record rather than a novel theoretical framework proposed within the text itself, though this is mitigated by the strong methodological innovation.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive' nature of the ideas relies heavily on the applicant's existing track record rather than a novel theoretical framework proposed within the text itself, though this is mitigated by the strong methodological innovation.

tor Award in 2018.
Impact of an Advanced Fellowship on my career:
I am committed to improving the quality of care for people with chronic rheumatic diseases. An NIHR Advanced
Fellowship will provide me with the best possible opportunity of achieving this goal and of becoming a leader in
data-driven quality improvement. It will help to secure me a tenured position at King’s College London, and allow
me to dedicate 80% of my time to research. Building on my combined experience in big data analysis, quality
improvement and clinical rheumatology, I would be at the forefront of using electronic health data to optimise
patient care. My research is highly relevant to organisations including NICE, HQIP and NHS England. The
expertise I develop during this Fellowship will enable me to establish myself as a leading academic collaborator in
this field, supported by a training programme that spans health data science, complex intervention development
and pragmatic trial design. No other Fellowship programme provides me this level of opportunity.
The PREVENT Study: Preventing hospital admissions attributable to

Proposed training and development programme
Training in skills necessary to support the proposed research
My NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and Clinical Lectureship have provided me with a strong foundation in big data
analysis, epidemiology and implementation science. An Advanced Fellowship would give me the opportunity to
develop new skills that will be incredibly helpful for this research proposal and in my future career as an
independent researcher in data-driven quality improvement:
 In Years 1 and 2, I will complete a part-time MSc in Health Data Science at the London School of
Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. This course aligns closely with the skills I will be using in Workstreams 1
and 3 of my proposal. It will be a fantastic opportunity to advance my skills as a health data scientist,
including modules dedicated to machine learning, predictive modelling and Bayesian analysis.
 In Year 2, I will complete the Developing and Evaluating Complex Interventions course at the
National Centre for Research Methods, University of Southampton. This course is led by Dr Kathyrn
Skivington, lead author of the MRC/NIHR Complex Intervention Development Framework, which I will be
using to develo

owship, I will complete a 3-month placement (1 day a week) with software developers
at the Bennett Institute for Applied Data Science, University of Oxford. This will give me the opportunity to
learn one-on-one from the team of professional coders, database engineers and data visualisation experts behind
the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment (led by Professor Ben Goldacre), which I will be utilising in
Workstreams 1 and 3.
Throughout my Fellowship, I will be working closely with the Data Analytics and Implementation Support teams
at NICE to develop the infrastructure needed to monitor the real-world uptake of clinical guidelines. This will be an
incredible learning and career development opportunity. It will enable me to influence the development and
implementation of national guidelines across numerous disease areas.
Training to support my development as a future health research leader
I will apply to the Emerging Research Leaders stream of NIHR’s Future-Focused Leadership programme,
which is designed specifically for post-doctoral researchers taking up their first significant leadership and
management roles. Through a mixture of residential workshops and training modules, I

re for people with gout and other crystal
arthropathies, which aligns closely with the aims of my Fellowship. There is a huge clinical need for this service,
with very few dedicated clinics in the UK for people with severe gout (e.g. those with end-stage renal disease,
severe tophaceous gout or recurrent hospitalisations). It will be fantastic opportunity to establish and lead such a

RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1
Name Professor James Galloway
Institution King's College London
Position Professor of Rheumatology
Description of support provided
James Galloway is a Professor of Rheumatology at King’s College London and Honorary Consultant
Rheumatologist at King’s College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust. He is the lead methodologist for the National
Early Inflammatory Arthritis Audit in England and Wales; methodology advisor to the British Society for
Rheumatology; and Deputy Director of the King’s College London Clinical Research Facility. Professor Galloway’s
expertise in epidemiology, quality improvement and clinical trials, make him ideally placed to support my research
proposal and career development.
Professor Galloway has been a fantastic mentor to me throughout my clini
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
cal academic career. He was my
primary supervisor during my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, and is the academic supervisor for my NIHR Clinical
Lectureship. He has been incredibly supportive of me in developing my own research niche in data-driven quality
improvement, and in offering me his time and expertise as I developed this Advanced Fellowship proposal.
Professor Galloway is ideally placed to support my development into an independent health researcher. He has
extensive experience as a supervisor and early career mentor, and he is Programme Director for Integrated
Academic Training at King’s College London. He has several senior management roles within higher education,
and collaborates closely with national organisations including NHS England, CQC and HQIP, to whom my
research is closely aligned. Importantly, Professor Galloway is familiar with the challenges of navigating clinical
work as an NHS rheumatologist, alongside a successful academic career.

sub_criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly articulates why this award is the 'best possible opportunity' to secure tenure and transition to independence (g.2.a, g.2.b). The proposal clearly details how the award builds on existing experience in rheumatology and data science while enabling new skills in complex intervention design and cluster trials (g.2.c). Partnerships with NICE, NHS England, and patient organizations are well-integrated to develop research networks (g.2.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The description of how the award will develop partnerships with industry or charity organizations is less detailed compared to the strong NHS and academic partnerships described.
sub_criteria.rationale: The description of how the award will develop partnerships with industry or charity organizations is less detailed compared to the strong NHS and academic partnerships described.

sub_criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant has extensive prior experience in big data analysis, implementation science, and RCTs (g.3.a), supported by formal training in epidemiology and statistics (g.3.b). There is a perfect alignment between this prior experience and the skills required for the proposed project, which utilizes OpenSAFELY and cluster RCTs (g.3.c).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for relevant experience is robust and comprehensive.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for relevant experience is robust and comprehensive.

Please describe your research career to date
I am a rheumatologist and NIHR Clinical Lecturer with a strong track record in health data analysis and quality
improvement.
Key research experience:
I was awarded an NIHR Doctoral Fellowship at King’s College London (KCL) in 2020. I combined big data analysis
(OpenSAFELY; CPRD; Hospital Episode Statistics) and implementation science methodology, to improve the
quality of care for people hospitalised for gout. During my Fellowship, I published 28 peer-reviewed publications,
including 4 first-author publications in The Lancet specialty journals. I was awarded prizes nationally (Royal
Society of Medicine, British Society for Rheumatology) and regionally (King’s Research Excellence Award). I was
interviewed by the BBC, The Times and The Guardian, which helped raise the profile of gout as a sub-optimally
managed condition.
Currently, I am an NIHR Clinical Lecturer at KCL, leading a project with the Healthcare Quality Improvement
Partnership (HQIP) to transform National C

linical Audit programmes in England and Wales using big data (Russell
et al. Lancet Rheumatology. https://doi.org/10.1016/S2665-9913(22)00305-8). I am co-investigator on an NIHR
HSDR grant (£2.3 million), led by Professor Laura Coates (University of Oxford), in which I am evaluating the real-
world effectiveness of the NHS England Patient-Initiated Follow-Up programme using electronic health record data
(OpenSAFELY; Hospital Episode Statistics). This project includes a pragmatic, multi-centre RCT (30 sites), which
provides me with an excellent grounding in the skillsets I will be using in this Advanced Fellowship.
In January 2024, I was awarded a competitive, industry-funded grant to fund 6 months of post-doctoral research. I
used National Clinical Audit data to demonstrate that under-served groups (particularly ethnic minorities and older
adults) are much less likely to receive biologic medications for rheumatoid arthritis than other patients (under peer-
review, Lancet Rheumatology).
Teaching and supervisory experience:
I thoroughly enjoy teaching, and regularly deliver clinically-focused seminars and lectures on Quality Improvement
for the MBBS programme at KCL. I have lectured

sub_criteria.name: Impactful Research Outputs and Outcomes
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful research outputs or outcomes appropriate to the applicant’s career stage, demonstrating contribution to the field.

signals.signal_text: Number and quality of publications, reports, or presentations
Citation metrics or other indicators of impact (where mentioned)
Awards, grants, or recognitions linked to prior research
Description of practical or societal impact of previous work

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant lists numerous high-impact publications in top journals like The Lancet Rheumatology and Rheumatology (g.4.a), including a guideline cited as the most-cited manuscript in 2023 (g.4.b). Multiple awards, such as the Eric Bywaters Prize and BSR Young Investigator Award, demonstrate recognition (g.4.c). The practical impact of previous work on national guidelines is clearly described (g.4.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While citation metrics are mentioned for one specific guideline, broader citation impact metrics for the entire portfolio are not explicitly quantified beyond the journal prestige.
sub_criteria.rationale: While citation metrics are mentioned for one specific guideline, broader citation impact metrics for the entire portfolio are not explicitly quantified beyond the journal prestige.

Please describe your research career to date
I am a rheumatologist and NIHR Clinical Lecturer with a strong track record in health data analysis and quality
improvement.
Key research experience:
I was awarded an NIHR Doctoral Fellowship at King’s College London (KCL) in 2020. I combined big data analysis
(OpenSAFELY; CPRD; Hospital Episode Statistics) and implementation science methodology, to improve the
quality of care for people hospitalised for gout. During my Fellowship, I published 28 peer-reviewed publications,
including 4 first-author publications in The Lancet specialty journals. I was awarded prizes nationally (Royal
Society of Medicine, British Society for Rheumatology) and regionally (King’s Research Excellence Award). I was
interviewed by the BBC, The Times and The Guardian, which helped raise the profile of gout as a sub-optimally
managed condition.
Currently, I am an NIHR Clinical Lecturer at KCL, leading a project with the Healthcare Quality Improvement
Partnership (HQIP) to transform National C

actice Award by the British Society for Rheumatology, and has been implemented at three other hospitals
nationally (Guy’s and St Thomas’ Hospital, Princess Royal University Hospital, Stockport NHS Foundation Trust).
During my Doctoral Fellowship, I was given the opportunity to lead the evidence review for a national guideline on
prescribing immunomodulatory drugs in pregnancy, commissioned by the British Society for Rheumatology. I
synthesised evidence from over 27,000 articles, managed a team of 4 evidence reviewers, and provided
methodological oversight. I liaised with a working group of 24 multi-disciplinary experts and patient
representatives. I published the guideline as first author (most-cited manuscript in Rheumatology,
2023: https://doi.org/10.1093/rheumatology/keac551), and I am regularly in correspondence about the implications
of the guideline. I was invited by the British Society for Rheumatology to support rheumatology trainees in taking
on guideline roles, and I have been invited to join the working group of another national guideline.
Patient and public engagement:
Patient involvement has been fundamental to the success of my doctoral and post-doctoral research - th

sub_criteria.name: Trajectory Towards Research Leadership
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates potential and progression towards becoming a future leader in health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Statements of career ambitions and long-term goals
Evidence of increasing responsibility in previous roles (e.g., leading projects, mentoring)
Participation in leadership or professional development programs
Recognition by peers or institutions suggesting leadership potential

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant clearly states career ambitions to become a leader in data-driven quality improvement and secure a tenured position (g.5.a). Evidence of increasing responsibility is shown through progression from trainee to Principal Investigator on grants and leading national guideline development (g.5.b). Participation in the NIHR Future-Focused Leadership programme and teaching roles demonstrates leadership development (g.5.c). Recognition by peers through awards supports leadership potential (g.5.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the trajectory towards leadership is well-documented with concrete examples of increasing responsibility.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the trajectory towards leadership is well-documented with concrete examples of increasing responsibility.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Practitioner-Academic Career (if applicable)
sub_criteria.definition: For applicants including a clinical or practice element, shows a clear commitment to combining research with professional practice.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of practitioner-academic career intentions
Involvement in clinical or practice-based research
Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities
Participation in relevant professional networks or qualifications

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly commits to a practitioner-academic career, evidenced by dual roles as a Rheumatology Specialist Trainee and NIHR Clinical Lecturer (g.6.a). Involvement in clinical research and teaching is extensive (g.6.b). The proposal includes a plan to balance 20% clinical time with research, including establishing a new clinic (g.6.c). Participation in professional networks like the UK Gout Society is noted (g.6.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The specific mechanisms for balancing the increasing clinical workload of a consultant role with the demands of a 5-year fellowship could be more detailed, though the 20% allocation is a strong start.
sub_criteria.rationale: The specific mechanisms for balancing the increasing clinical workload of a consultant role with the demands of a 5-year fellowship could be more detailed, though the 20% allocation is a strong start.

criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision to become an independent leader in data-driven quality improvement (g.1.a) and a distinctive direction combining big data with implementation science (g.1.b). The training plan is exceptionally well-justified with specific courses mapped to workstreams (g.1.c), and strong mentorship is evidenced by three named supervisors with complementary expertise and a structured meeting framework (g.1.d).
criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive' nature of the ideas relies heavily on the applicant's existing track record rather than a novel theoretical framework proposed within the text itself, though this is mitigated by the strong methodological innovation.
criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive' nature of the ideas relies heavily on the applicant's existing track record rather than a novel theoretical framework proposed within the text itself, though this is mitigated by the strong methodological innovation.

evidence.text:
tor Award in 2018.
Impact of an Advanced Fellowship on my career:
I am committed to improving the quality of care for people with chronic rheumatic diseases. An NIHR Advanced
Fellowship will provide me with the best possible opportunity of achieving this goal and of becoming a leader in
data-driven quality improvement. It will help to secure me a tenured position at King’s College London, and allow
me to dedicate 80% of my time to research. Building on my combined experience in big data analysis, quality
improvement and clinical rheumatology, I would be at the forefront of using electronic health data to optimise
patient care. My research is highly relevant to organisations including NICE, HQIP and NHS England. The
expertise I develop during this Fellowship will enable me to establish myself as a leading academic collaborator in
this field, supported by a training programme that spans health data science, complex intervention development
and pragmatic trial design. No other Fellowship programme provides me this level of opportunity.
The PREVENT Study: Preventing hospital admissions attributable to
Title of research degree
gout
Has the degree already
Yes
been awarded?
Date of Award

Proposed training and development programme
Training in skills necessary to support the proposed research
My NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and Clinical Lectureship have provided me with a strong foundation in big data
analysis, epidemiology and implementation science. An Advanced Fellowship would give me the opportunity to
develop new skills that will be incredibly helpful for this research proposal and in my future career as an
independent researcher in data-driven quality improvement:
 In Years 1 and 2, I will complete a part-time MSc in Health Data Science at the London School of
Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. This course aligns closely with the skills I will be using in Workstreams 1
and 3 of my proposal. It will be a fantastic opportunity to advance my skills as a health data scientist,
including modules dedicated to machine learning, predictive modelling and Bayesian analysis.
 In Year 2, I will complete the Developing and Evaluating Complex Interventions course at the
National Centre for Research Methods, University of Southampton. This course is led by Dr Kathyrn
Skivington, lead author of the MRC/NIHR Complex Intervention Development Framework, which I will be
using to develo
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
owship, I will complete a 3-month placement (1 day a week) with software developers
at the Bennett Institute for Applied Data Science, University of Oxford. This will give me the opportunity to
learn one-on-one from the team of professional coders, database engineers and data visualisation experts behind
the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment (led by Professor Ben Goldacre), which I will be utilising in
Workstreams 1 and 3.
Throughout my Fellowship, I will be working closely with the Data Analytics and Implementation Support teams
at NICE to develop the infrastructure needed to monitor the real-world uptake of clinical guidelines. This will be an
incredible learning and career development opportunity. It will enable me to influence the development and
implementation of national guidelines across numerous disease areas.
Training to support my development as a future health research leader
I will apply to the Emerging Research Leaders stream of NIHR’s Future-Focused Leadership programme,
which is designed specifically for post-doctoral researchers taking up their first significant leadership and
management roles. Through a mixture of residential workshops and training modules, I
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
re for people with gout and other crystal
arthropathies, which aligns closely with the aims of my Fellowship. There is a huge clinical need for this service,
with very few dedicated clinics in the UK for people with severe gout (e.g. those with end-stage renal disease,
severe tophaceous gout or recurrent hospitalisations). It will be fantastic opportunity to establish and lead such a
service.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1
Name Professor James Galloway
Institution King's College London
Position Professor of Rheumatology
Description of support provided
James Galloway is a Professor of Rheumatology at King’s College London and Honorary Consultant
Rheumatologist at King’s College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust. He is the lead methodologist for the National
Early Inflammatory Arthritis Audit in England and Wales; methodology advisor to the British Society for
Rheumatology; and Deputy Director of the King’s College London Clinical Research Facility. Professor Galloway’s
expertise in epidemiology, quality improvement and clinical trials, make him ideally placed to support my research
proposal and career development.
Professor Galloway has been a fantastic mentor to me throughout my clini
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
cal academic career. He was my
primary supervisor during my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, and is the academic supervisor for my NIHR Clinical
Lectureship. He has been incredibly supportive of me in developing my own research niche in data-driven quality
improvement, and in offering me his time and expertise as I developed this Advanced Fellowship proposal.
Professor Galloway is ideally placed to support my development into an independent health researcher. He has
extensive experience as a supervisor and early career mentor, and he is Programme Director for Integrated
Academic Training at King’s College London. He has several senior management roles within higher education,
and collaborates closely with national organisations including NHS England, CQC and HQIP, to whom my
research is closely aligned. Importantly, Professor Galloway is familiar with the challenges of navigating clinical
work as an NHS rheumatologist, alongside a successful academic career.

Institution University of Oxford
Position NIHR Clinician Scientist
Description of support provided

criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly articulates why this award is the 'best possible opportunity' to secure tenure and transition to independence (g.2.a, g.2.b). The proposal clearly details how the award builds on existing experience in rheumatology and data science while enabling new skills in complex intervention design and cluster trials (g.2.c). Partnerships with NICE, NHS England, and patient organizations are well-integrated to develop research networks (g.2.d).
criteria.drawbacks: The description of how the award will develop partnerships with industry or charity organizations is less detailed compared to the strong NHS and academic partnerships described.
criteria.rationale: The description of how the award will develop partnerships with industry or charity organizations is less detailed compared to the strong NHS and academic partnerships described.

criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

criteria.pros: The applicant has extensive prior experience in big data analysis, implementation science, and RCTs (g.3.a), supported by formal training in epidemiology and statistics (g.3.b). There is a perfect alignment between this prior experience and the skills required for the proposed project, which utilizes OpenSAFELY and cluster RCTs (g.3.c).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for relevant experience is robust and comprehensive.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for relevant experience is robust and comprehensive.

Please describe your research career to date
I am a rheumatologist and NIHR Clinical Lecturer with a strong track record in health data analysis and quality
improvement.
Key research experience:
I was awarded an NIHR Doctoral Fellowship at King’s College London (KCL) in 2020. I combined big data analysis
(OpenSAFELY; CPRD; Hospital Episode Statistics) and implementation science methodology, to improve the
quality of care for people hospitalised for gout. During my Fellowship, I published 28 peer-reviewed publications,
including 4 first-author publications in The Lancet specialty journals. I was awarded prizes nationally (Royal
Society of Medicine, British Society for Rheumatology) and regionally (King’s Research Excellence Award). I was
interviewed by the BBC, The Times and The Guardian, which helped raise the profile of gout as a sub-optimally
managed condition.
Currently, I am an NIHR Clinical Lecturer at KCL, leading a project with the Healthcare Quality Improvement
Partnership (HQIP) to transform National C

linical Audit programmes in England and Wales using big data (Russell
et al. Lancet Rheumatology. https://doi.org/10.1016/S2665-9913(22)00305-8). I am co-investigator on an NIHR
HSDR grant (£2.3 million), led by Professor Laura Coates (University of Oxford), in which I am evaluating the real-
world effectiveness of the NHS England Patient-Initiated Follow-Up programme using electronic health record data
(OpenSAFELY; Hospital Episode Statistics). This project includes a pragmatic, multi-centre RCT (30 sites), which
provides me with an excellent grounding in the skillsets I will be using in this Advanced Fellowship.
In January 2024, I was awarded a competitive, industry-funded grant to fund 6 months of post-doctoral research. I
used National Clinical Audit data to demonstrate that under-served groups (particularly ethnic minorities and older
adults) are much less likely to receive biologic medications for rheumatoid arthritis than other patients (under peer-
review, Lancet Rheumatology).
Teaching and supervisory experience:
I thoroughly enjoy teaching, and regularly deliver clinically-focused seminars and lectures on Quality Improvement
for the MBBS programme at KCL. I have lectured

criteria.name: Trajectory Towards Research Leadership
criteria.definition: Demonstrates potential and progression towards becoming a future leader in health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Statements of career ambitions and long-term goals
Evidence of increasing responsibility in previous roles (e.g., leading projects, mentoring)
Participation in leadership or professional development programs
Recognition by peers or institutions suggesting leadership potential

criteria.pros: The applicant clearly states career ambitions to become a leader in data-driven quality improvement and secure a tenured position (g.5.a). Evidence of increasing responsibility is shown through progression from trainee to Principal Investigator on grants and leading national guideline development (g.5.b). Participation in the NIHR Future-Focused Leadership programme and teaching roles demonstrates leadership development (g.5.c). Recognition by peers through awards supports leadership potential (g.5.d).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the trajectory towards leadership is well-documented with concrete examples of increasing responsibility.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the trajectory towards leadership is well-documented with concrete examples of increasing responsibility.

criteria.name: Commitment to Practitioner-Academic Career (if applicable)
criteria.definition: For applicants including a clinical or practice element, shows a clear commitment to combining research with professional practice.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of practitioner-academic career intentions
Involvement in clinical or practice-based research
Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities
Participation in relevant professional networks or qualifications

criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly commits to a practitioner-academic career, evidenced by dual roles as a Rheumatology Specialist Trainee and NIHR Clinical Lecturer (g.6.a). Involvement in clinical research and teaching is extensive (g.6.b). The proposal includes a plan to balance 20% clinical time with research, including establishing a new clinic (g.6.c). Participation in professional networks like the UK Gout Society is noted (g.6.d).
criteria.drawbacks: The specific mechanisms for balancing the increasing clinical workload of a consultant role with the demands of a 5-year fellowship could be more detailed, though the 20% allocation is a strong start.
criteria.rationale: The specific mechanisms for balancing the increasing clinical workload of a consultant role with the demands of a 5-year fellowship could be more detailed, though the 20% allocation is a strong start.

sub_criteria.name: Quality of the Plain English Summary
sub_criteria.definition: Clear, simple description of the research that a non-specialist audience can understand; explains problem, importance, plan, beneficiaries, and real-world use; avoids jargon; aligns with the detailed proposal.

signals.signal_text: Readability scores (e.g., Flesch-Kincaid)
Low density of unexplained jargon or technical terms
Presence of problem statement, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact
Alignment with detailed proposal content
Sentence structure and coherence for non-specialist understanding
Emphasis on societal or patient relevance

sub_criteria.pros: The Plain English Summary demonstrates excellent readability with a Flesch-Kincaid grade of 7.73 and no long sentences (secpena__001). It clearly articulates the problem, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact in accessible language (secpesor__001_a, secpesor__001_c). The content aligns well with the detailed proposal, covering key elements like the use of OpenSAFELY data and the cluster RCT (secpesor__001_b).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the jargon density is low (3.47%), some technical terms like 'allopurinol' and 'cluster randomised' are present, though they are generally explained in context. evidence.text:
Why is this research needed?
Clinical guidelines are recommendations on how to diagnose and treat medical conditions, based upon the best
available evidence. However, we cannot easily check whether these recommendations are being followed in real-
world care. Current methods (e.g. collecting data manually using spreadsheets) take too long and make it hard to
build a national picture of care quality.
Importantly, we can now bring together electronic health records for the whole population of England using a
system called OpenSAFELY. This does not need manual data collection, and patients cannot be identified. It gives
us a unique opportunity to change how we monitor the quality of care for patients.
What are the aims of my research?
My goal is to improve patient care by increasing the use of guideline recommendations. To achieve this, I will do
the following:
1. Create a data-driven way of monitoring guideline use in real-world care;
2. Use this information to make a plan that gives guideline-recommended care to patients;
3. Test this plan using real-world data in OpenSAFELY.
To show this, I have chosen a condition with very effective treatments, where guidelines are rarely followed

these complications, but they are not used often enough or in the right amounts.
What will my project do?
1. Check guideline use: I will use anonymous GP and hospital data for 58 million people in OpenSAFELY to
see whether national treatment recommendations are being followed for people with gout. I will show how

how this affects health outcomes.

makers, to find areas of good care and areas for improvement. Together, we will make a plan that gives
guideline-recommended care to people with gout, using digital solutions and education.
3. Real-world testing: I will test whether this plan improves patient care using an efficient, real-world study in
OpenSAFELY. General practices (38 in total) will provide care to people with gout using either the new

openly through conferences, research papers, and by working with our professional and patient communities.

sub_criteria.pros: The application critically reviews existing literature, identifying specific limitations of manual audits and current data platforms like CPRD (secdrp__001_e). It clearly identifies knowledge gaps regarding the implementation of NICE guidance in gout care and justifies the use of OpenSAFELY to address these gaps (secdrp__001_f, secdrp__001_h). The review provides a logical flow leading to the proposed study design.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The evidence review is strong but focused primarily on the specific context of gout and data availability; a broader review of implementation science frameworks beyond the MRC framework might have been beneficial, though the MRC framework is explicitly cited as the basis for Workstream 2.
sub_criteria.rationale: The evidence review is strong but focused primarily on the specific context of gout and data availability; a broader review of implementation science frameworks beyond the MRC framework might have been beneficial, though the MRC framework is explicitly cited as the basis for Workstream 2.

openly to support efficient re-use.
3. Existing evidence
Existing approaches for monitoring the real-world uptake of guideline recommendations rely heavily upon manual
audit,3 performance.15,16
clinical which is labour-intensive and provides only a snapshot of National Audit
programmes can help to build a national picture of care quality, but with several limitations:
 No data are captured on patients not enrolled in these audits;1
quality;4
 Missing data varies markedly by healthcare provider, and associates with poor care
 National Audit programmes are absent for many conditions (e.g. gout).
Electronic health record data can overcome many of these limitations. Clinically-coded information on
diagnoses, investigations and prescriptions can be linked across multiple data sources and analysed to evaluate
care quality. Existing data platforms (e.g. CPRD) have been available for over 20 years; however, their suitability
for evaluating guideline implementation is limited: data are often outdated (e.g. >2 years for hospital data) and are
present for only a minority of the population.18,19
The introduction of the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research E

nvironment during the COVID-19 pandemic has
transformed our ability to deliver health data analyses for the whole of England:
 There is data coverage for >99% of England’s population;20
 Primary care data are updated weekly, and secondary care data monthly;20
 There is linkage to numerous additional datasets, including high-cost drugs and referral-to-treatment
times;20
 Privacy safeguards are greater than for any other platform.20,53
In my pilot work, I showed that OpenSAFELY can be used to evaluate the impact of the pandemic on long-
term conditions.1,2 I demonstrated that inflammatory arthritis diagnoses fell by 40% during the early pandemic,
while highlighting the remarkable adaptation of the NHS in the face of unprecedented pressures.1,2 These
analyses required no manual data collection; the number of captured diagnoses was 5-times greater than the
existing (manually-collected) National Audit programme;22 and these analyses can be updated regularly using
electronic dashboards linked to OpenSAFELY.
NHS England recently expanded OpenSAFELY’s use beyond COVID-19-related projects,23 making this
proposal timely and relevant. I will use this incredible resource to develop a data-

te this in near real-time, highlighting variation between regions and
patient groups, and quantifying the impact on health outcomes.
What is also clear from existing evidence is that guidelines need to be accompanied by effective
implementation strategies.10,26 I showed that guidelines from specialist societies (BSR, EULAR) had no
meaningful impact on care for people with gout in the UK.10 A recent systematic review highlighted that nurse-led
education approaches can improve gout care in randomised-controlled trial settings, assuming sufficient
available.17,21
resources are However, this is unlikely to be scalable within the resource envelope of the NHS – a
challenge I encountered when trying to obtain NHS funding to sustain the nurse-led, hospital gout management
pathway I developed in my Doctoral Fellowship.
Some of the most effective strategies for implementing complex healthcare interventions are digital solutions that
streamline care and reduce unnecessary workload.27 Evidence from small studies outside the UK have shown
that electronic health record solutions (e.g. decision-support tools and electronic prompts) can improve outcomes
for people with gout.28-30 Despite their pot
