evidence.text:
methodology,
I have chosen gout as an exemplar disease with a persistent evidence-practice gap.
I will complete three workstreams:
1. Develop a reproducible analytic framework to monitor the implementation of NICE guidance;
2. Design a multi-faceted intervention to maximise uptake of guideline-recommended care;
3. Evaluate this intervention using a data-enabled, cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY.
Methods (timelines for delivery)
Workstream 1 (months 0-12): Using anonymised GP and hospital data for 58 million people in OpenSAFELY, I will
systematically map recommendations from the NICE Gout Guideline and analyse temporal trends in their
implementation. I will use regression-modelling approaches to identify predictors of sub-optimal care, and evaluate
the impact on outcomes including comorbidities and hospitalisations. Data-driven dashboards will feedback
performance against NICE standards at national, regional and local levels.
Workstream 2 (months 12-24): I will convene focus groups with broad stakeholder representation to identify
barriers and facilitators of optimal gout care. I will collaborate with implementation science experts at King's
College London to develop an evid

ence-based intervention package that provides NICE-recommended care for
people with gout, incorporating digital solutions within electronic health record software and educational content.
Workstream 3 (months 24-60): I will conduct a data-enabled, cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY to evaluate
the intervention from Workstream 2. General practices (n=38) will be randomised 1:1 to receive this intervention or
usual care. All study data will be automatically captured in OpenSAFELY and analysed using the framework
developed in Workstream 1. Outcomes will include:
 The uptake of NICE-recommended gout treatments (e.g. allopurinol);
 The incidence of gout-associated comorbidities (e.g. kidney disease);
 Consultation/hospitalisation rates.
Anticipated impact
My research will directly benefit patients with gout, by reducing pain and disability. Better disease control will
prevent avoidable hospitalisations and complications. For policy makers, it will help target strategies towards
reducing healthcare inequality. I will be collaborating with NICE throughout my Fellowship to ensure this
methodology is readily adaptable for other guidelines and diseases. I will share all of my analysis

mendations and
outcomes using health data for >99% of England’s population.
2. Develop a multi-faceted intervention to maximise the uptake of NICE guidance, incorporating digital
solutions and education.
3. Evaluate the implementation and effectiveness of this intervention using an efficient, data-enabled,
cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY.
5. Project plan
Workstream 1 (months 0-12): Monitoring NICE guidance implementation
Aim
To develop a data-driven approach to monitoring real-world uptake of NICE guidance, highlighting variation in
care, and evaluating impact for patients.
I will develop this methodology using the NICE guideline for the management of gout. I am collaborating with
NICE to ensure the methodology is readily adaptable for other guidelines and diseases.
Methods
Data sources: I will use anonymised electronic health record data from primary and secondary care for >99% of
England’s population, linked securely via OpenSAFELY. I will publish my analysis code (Python/Stata/Markdown)
via GitHub to enable efficient re-use.
Study design: I will use an observational cohort design to evaluate the uptake and impact of NICE
recommendations for all people with recorded gout d

sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks identified; the methodological justification is comprehensive and well-supported by the applicant's training plan.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks identified; the methodological justification is comprehensive and well-supported by the applicant's training plan.

Society, Versus Arthritis), professional organisations (British Society for Rheumatology, Royal College of General

1. Plain language summaries to accompany all publications, co-written with my patient advisory group;
2. Social media summaries;
3. Public engagement events, via NIHR, RCGP and BSR networks;
4. Research open-days and science festivals;
5. Newsletters.
Impact statement: The goal of my Fellowship is to improve patient care by maximising the uptake of guideline-
recommended care:
 My research will directly benefit patients with gout, through reductions in pain, disability and
complications (e.g. erosive joint damage and kidney disease).
 For NHS clinicians, the intervention I develop will reduce unnecessary workload and serve as a
framework for delivering best practice care for other diseases.
 For commissioners, better disease control will reduce the number of avoidable consultations,
hospitalisations and complications.

see whether national treatment recommendations are being followed for people with gout. I will show how
care varies throughout England; find out what determines whether patients receive good quality care; and
how this affects health outcomes.
2. Make a plan to improve care: I will work with patients, healthcare professionals, charities and policy
makers, to find areas of good care and areas for improvement. Together, we will make a plan that gives
guideline-recommended care to people with gout, using digital solutions and education.
3. Real-world testing: I will test whether this plan improves patient care using an efficient, real-world study in
OpenSAFELY. General practices (38 in total) will provide care to people with gout using either the new

How will the results be used?
My research will directly benefit people with gout, by reducing pain and disability. It will help doctors and nurses

evidence.text:
methodology,
I have chosen gout as an exemplar disease with a persistent evidence-practice gap.
I will complete three workstreams:
1. Develop a reproducible analytic framework to monitor the implementation of NICE guidance;
2. Design a multi-faceted intervention to maximise uptake of guideline-recommended care;
3. Evaluate this intervention using a data-enabled, cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY.
Methods (timelines for delivery)
Workstream 1 (months 0-12): Using anonymised GP and hospital data for 58 million people in OpenSAFELY, I will
systematically map recommendations from the NICE Gout Guideline and analyse temporal trends in their
implementation. I will use regression-modelling approaches to identify predictors of sub-optimal care, and evaluate
the impact on outcomes including comorbidities and hospitalisations. Data-driven dashboards will feedback
performance against NICE standards at national, regional and local levels.
Workstream 2 (months 12-24): I will convene focus groups with broad stakeholder representation to identify
barriers and facilitators of optimal gout care. I will collaborate with implementation science experts at King's
College London to develop an evid

code openly, and
communicate my findings through conferences, research papers, social media, and PPIE events.

Methods
Stakeholders: I will establish a core stakeholder group, who I will consult throughout the stages listed below,
including people with gout, healthcare professionals, commissioners and third-sector representatives.
Focus groups: Three focus groups will be convened to encourage open discussion among those with shared
experiences and develop a shared understanding of the challenges of achieving optimal gout care:
 Focus group one will include people with gout, carers and relatives. I will use the links I have formed with
patient organisations (including the UK Gout Society) to purposively sample patients from diverse
backgrounds, including ethnic minority and under-served communities, older patients, and those with
multiple long-term conditions.

implementation and effectiveness of the intervention from Workstream 2. All study data will be automatically

be required.
This methodology will be readily adaptable for other interventions and diseases, and will be shared openly
including with my collaborators at NICE.
Methods
Study design: Two-arm, parallel-group, cluster-randomised trial, with general practices as the unit of
randomisation. I have selected this study design as the intervention will be delivered at practice-level, reducing
treatment contamination between individuals.
Target population: People aged ≥18 years, registered with general practices in England, who consult a primary
care clinician for gout. Every patient consulting for gout at participating practices will be eligible; there will be no
exclusion criteria.

The intervention package from Workstream 2; or

evidence.text:
Why is this research needed?
Clinical guidelines are recommendations on how to diagnose and treat medical conditions, based upon the best
available evidence. However, we cannot easily check whether these recommendations are being followed in real-
world care. Current methods (e.g. collecting data manually using spreadsheets) take too long and make it hard to
build a national picture of care quality.
Importantly, we can now bring together electronic health records for the whole population of England using a
system called OpenSAFELY. This does not need manual data collection, and patients cannot be identified. It gives
us a unique opportunity to change how we monitor the quality of care for patients.
What are the aims of my research?
My goal is to improve patient care by increasing the use of guideline recommendations. To achieve this, I will do
the following:
1. Create a data-driven way of monitoring guideline use in real-world care;
2. Use this information to make a plan that gives guideline-recommended care to patients;
3. Test this plan using real-world data in OpenSAFELY.
To show this, I have chosen a condition with very effective treatments, where guidelines are rarely followed
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
. Gout
is a common type of arthritis, affecting 1 in 40 adults. It causes severe pain, permanent joint damage and health
problems (e.g. kidney disease) if not treated well enough. Very good medications (e.g. allopurinol) can prevent
these complications, but they are not used often enough or in the right amounts.
What will my project do?
1. Check guideline use: I will use anonymous GP and hospital data for 58 million people in OpenSAFELY to
see whether national treatment recommendations are being followed for people with gout. I will show how
care varies throughout England; find out what determines whether patients receive good quality care; and
how this affects health outcomes.
2. Make a plan to improve care: I will work with patients, healthcare professionals, charities and policy
makers, to find areas of good care and areas for improvement. Together, we will make a plan that gives
guideline-recommended care to people with gout, using digital solutions and education.
3. Real-world testing: I will test whether this plan improves patient care using an efficient, real-world study in
OpenSAFELY. General practices (38 in total) will provide care to people with gout using either the new
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

plan or the treatment that would usually be given. All study data will be collected automatically in
OpenSAFELY, with no extra data needed from patients or doctors. The following results will be compared
for patients treated for gout over a 12-month period:
Whether patients received guideline-recommended treatments (e.g. allopurinol);
o
If they developed complications of gout (e.g. kidney problems);
o
o How often they visited their GP or hospital.
How will the results be used?
My research will directly benefit people with gout, by reducing pain and disability. It will help doctors and nurses
give better care to their patients. It will reduce costs for the NHS and help focus resources on areas where
improvement is needed most. I will be working closely with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
(NICE) to make sure my research benefits people with other long-term conditions. I will share all of my findings
openly through conferences, research papers, and by working with our professional and patient communities.

criteria.definition: How comprehensively and critically the application reviews existing literature and evidence.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of references cited
Presence of critical analysis vs. mere summary
Identification of knowledge gaps
Coherence and logical flow

criteria.pros: The application critically reviews existing literature, identifying specific limitations of manual audits and current data platforms like CPRD (secdrp__001_e). It clearly identifies knowledge gaps regarding the implementation of NICE guidance in gout care and justifies the use of OpenSAFELY to address these gaps (secdrp__001_f, secdrp__001_h). The review provides a logical flow leading to the proposed study design.
criteria.drawbacks: The evidence review is strong but focused primarily on the specific context of gout and data availability; a broader review of implementation science frameworks beyond the MRC framework might have been beneficial, though the MRC framework is explicitly cited as the basis for Workstream 2.
criteria.rationale: The evidence review is strong but focused primarily on the specific context of gout and data availability; a broader review of implementation science frameworks beyond the MRC framework might have been beneficial, though the MRC framework is explicitly cited as the basis for Workstream 2.

evidence.text:
ill share my analysis code and methodology
openly to support efficient re-use.
3. Existing evidence
Existing approaches for monitoring the real-world uptake of guideline recommendations rely heavily upon manual
audit,3 performance.15,16
clinical which is labour-intensive and provides only a snapshot of National Audit
programmes can help to build a national picture of care quality, but with several limitations:
 No data are captured on patients not enrolled in these audits;1
quality;4
 Missing data varies markedly by healthcare provider, and associates with poor care
 National Audit programmes are absent for many conditions (e.g. gout).
Electronic health record data can overcome many of these limitations. Clinically-coded information on
diagnoses, investigations and prescriptions can be linked across multiple data sources and analysed to evaluate
care quality. Existing data platforms (e.g. CPRD) have been available for over 20 years; however, their suitability
for evaluating guideline implementation is limited: data are often outdated (e.g. >2 years for hospital data) and are
present for only a minority of the population.18,19
The introduction of the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research E

nvironment during the COVID-19 pandemic has
transformed our ability to deliver health data analyses for the whole of England:
 There is data coverage for >99% of England’s population;20
 Primary care data are updated weekly, and secondary care data monthly;20
 There is linkage to numerous additional datasets, including high-cost drugs and referral-to-treatment
times;20
 Privacy safeguards are greater than for any other platform.20,53
In my pilot work, I showed that OpenSAFELY can be used to evaluate the impact of the pandemic on long-
term conditions.1,2 I demonstrated that inflammatory arthritis diagnoses fell by 40% during the early pandemic,
while highlighting the remarkable adaptation of the NHS in the face of unprecedented pressures.1,2 These
analyses required no manual data collection; the number of captured diagnoses was 5-times greater than the
existing (manually-collected) National Audit programme;22 and these analyses can be updated regularly using
electronic dashboards linked to OpenSAFELY.
NHS England recently expanded OpenSAFELY’s use beyond COVID-19-related projects,23 making this
proposal timely and relevant. I will use this incredible resource to develop a data-

te this in near real-time, highlighting variation between regions and
patient groups, and quantifying the impact on health outcomes.
What is also clear from existing evidence is that guidelines need to be accompanied by effective
implementation strategies.10,26 I showed that guidelines from specialist societies (BSR, EULAR) had no
meaningful impact on care for people with gout in the UK.10 A recent systematic review highlighted that nurse-led
education approaches can improve gout care in randomised-controlled trial settings, assuming sufficient
available.17,21

criteria.definition: Whether the project scope matches the scale and expectations of the proposed award (doctoral, postdoctoral, etc.).

signals.signal_text: Alignment of aims with award level
Feasibility indicators (time, complexity, tasks)
Proportion of work relative to award expectations

criteria.pros: The project scope is highly appropriate for a 60-month fellowship, with three distinct workstreams clearly defined (secdrp__001_j). Feasibility is supported by specific sample sizes (38 practices, 1.45m patients), power calculations, and a realistic timeline (secdrp__001_l, secdrp__001_w). The Plain English Summary and Abstract reinforce this with concrete tasks and timelines (secpesor__001_b, secsa__001_b).
criteria.drawbacks: The scope is ambitious, involving a large-scale cluster RCT and complex data analysis; however, the applicant's prior experience and the detailed resource justification mitigate concerns about over-extension.
criteria.rationale: The scope is ambitious, involving a large-scale cluster RCT and complex data analysis; however, the applicant's prior experience and the detailed resource justification mitigate concerns about over-extension.

evidence.text:
. Gout
is a common type of arthritis, affecting 1 in 40 adults. It causes severe pain, permanent joint damage and health
problems (e.g. kidney disease) if not treated well enough. Very good medications (e.g. allopurinol) can prevent
these complications, but they are not used often enough or in the right amounts.
What will my project do?
1. Check guideline use: I will use anonymous GP and hospital data for 58 million people in OpenSAFELY to
see whether national treatment recommendations are being followed for people with gout. I will show how
care varies throughout England; find out what determines whether patients receive good quality care; and
how this affects health outcomes.
2. Make a plan to improve care: I will work with patients, healthcare professionals, charities and policy
makers, to find areas of good care and areas for improvement. Together, we will make a plan that gives
guideline-recommended care to people with gout, using digital solutions and education.
3. Real-world testing: I will test whether this plan improves patient care using an efficient, real-world study in
OpenSAFELY. General practices (38 in total) will provide care to people with gout using either the new

Whether patients received guideline-recommended treatments (e.g. allopurinol);
o

o
o How often they visited their GP or hospital.
How will the results be used?
My research will directly benefit people with gout, by reducing pain and disability. It will help doctors and nurses
give better care to their patients. It will reduce costs for the NHS and help focus resources on areas where
improvement is needed most. I will be working closely with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
(NICE) to make sure my research benefits people with other long-term conditions. I will share all of my findings
openly through conferences, research papers, and by working with our professional and patient communities.

evidence.text:
methodology,
I have chosen gout as an exemplar disease with a persistent evidence-practice gap.
I will complete three workstreams:
1. Develop a reproducible analytic framework to monitor the implementation of NICE guidance;
2. Design a multi-faceted intervention to maximise uptake of guideline-recommended care;
3. Evaluate this intervention using a data-enabled, cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY.
Methods (timelines for delivery)
Workstream 1 (months 0-12): Using anonymised GP and hospital data for 58 million people in OpenSAFELY, I will
systematically map recommendations from the NICE Gout Guideline and analyse temporal trends in their
implementation. I will use regression-modelling approaches to identify predictors of sub-optimal care, and evaluate
the impact on outcomes including comorbidities and hospitalisations. Data-driven dashboards will feedback
performance against NICE standards at national, regional and local levels.
Workstream 2 (months 12-24): I will convene focus groups with broad stakeholder representation to identify
barriers and facilitators of optimal gout care. I will collaborate with implementation science experts at King's
College London to develop an evid

ence-based intervention package that provides NICE-recommended care for
people with gout, incorporating digital solutions within electronic health record software and educational content.
Workstream 3 (months 24-60): I will conduct a data-enabled, cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY to evaluate
the intervention from Workstream 2. General practices (n=38) will be randomised 1:1 to receive this intervention or
usual care. All study data will be automatically captured in OpenSAFELY and analysed using the framework
developed in Workstream 1. Outcomes will include:
 The uptake of NICE-recommended gout treatments (e.g. allopurinol);
 The incidence of gout-associated comorbidities (e.g. kidney disease);
 Consultation/hospitalisation rates.
Anticipated impact
My research will directly benefit patients with gout, by reducing pain and disability. Better disease control will
prevent avoidable hospitalisations and complications. For policy makers, it will help target strategies towards
reducing healthcare inequality. I will be collaborating with NICE throughout my Fellowship to ensure this
methodology is readily adaptable for other guidelines and diseases. I will share all of my analysis

mendations and
outcomes using health data for >99% of England’s population.
2. Develop a multi-faceted intervention to maximise the uptake of NICE guidance, incorporating digital
solutions and education.
3. Evaluate the implementation and effectiveness of this intervention using an efficient, data-enabled,
cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY.
5. Project plan
Workstream 1 (months 0-12): Monitoring NICE guidance implementation
Aim
To develop a data-driven approach to monitoring real-world uptake of NICE guidance, highlighting variation in
care, and evaluating impact for patients.
I will develop this methodology using the NICE guideline for the management of gout. I am collaborating with
NICE to ensure the methodology is readily adaptable for other guidelines and diseases.
Methods
Data sources: I will use anonymised electronic health record data from primary and secondary care for >99% of
England’s population, linked securely via OpenSAFELY. I will publish my analysis code (Python/Stata/Markdown)
via GitHub to enable efficient re-use.
Study design: I will use an observational cohort design to evaluate the uptake and impact of NICE
recommendations for all people with recorded gout d

criteria.name: Research Design & Methodological Rigor
criteria.definition: Clarity, appropriateness, and robustness of study design to answer the research questions.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of methods
Logical alignment between aims and methods
Discussion of controls, sample size, or methodology justification
Structured methodological sections

criteria.pros: The research design is methodologically rigorous, explicitly detailing interrupted time-series analysis, cluster-randomised trial design, minimisation techniques, and handling of missing data (secdrp__001_k, secdrp__001_s). The methods align logically with the aims, utilizing OpenSAFELY for large-scale data analysis and a nested qualitative study for intervention development (secdrp__001_v). The Plain English Summary and Abstract accurately reflect these complex methods in accessible terms (secpesor__001_b, secsa__001_b).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks identified; the methodological justification is comprehensive and well-supported by the applicant's training plan.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks identified; the methodological justification is comprehensive and well-supported by the applicant's training plan.

criteria.name: Potential Impact on Patients, Public, and Health Services
criteria.definition: Likely benefits or influence of the research on patients, carers, or health and care systems.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of direct or indirect patient/public benefits
Alignment with health priorities or societal needs
Clarity and specificity of expected outcomes

criteria.pros: The application explicitly outlines significant benefits for patients (reduced pain, fewer complications), clinicians (reduced workload), and the NHS (cost savings, reduced inequality) (secdrp__001_d, secdrp__001_{). The impact is aligned with health priorities, specifically addressing healthcare inequality and the implementation of NICE guidance (secdrp__001_|). The Plain English Summary and Abstract clearly communicate these benefits (secpesor__001_c, secsa__001_c).
criteria.drawbacks: While the potential impact is high, the realization of cost savings and inequality reduction depends on the successful implementation of the intervention, which is a standard caveat for implementation research.
criteria.rationale: While the potential impact is high, the realization of cost savings and inequality reduction depends on the successful implementation of the intervention, which is a standard caveat for implementation research.

manual data. We know that data
gaps exist for under-served groups and under-resourced departments.4 By utilising routinely-captured data, we
can quality assure care more comprehensively and target interventions towards reducing healthcare inequality.
By developing this methodology for a disease with persistently sub-standard care, patients will directly benefit
from my research. Of 1.7 million people with gout in the UK, only a third are prescribed guideline-recommended
treatments.10,12 I will develop an intervention that delivers best practice care to patients, resulting in less pain,
fewer complications, and improved quality-of-life.
For healthcare professionals, the intervention will reduce unnecessary workload and enable them to achieve
better outcomes for their patients. Electronic dashboards will provide feedback on performance against NICE
standards – a powerful motivator for change.13 Better disease control will prevent avoidable hospitalisations and
complications, producing cost-savings for the NHS.14
The data-enabled trial design I am utilising will provide researchers with an efficient mechanism through which
complex interventions can be evaluated in real-world care. I w

g our health system and benefitting patients. I will use multiple approaches to inform study
participants, patients and the wider population, utilising collaborations with patient organisations (UK Gout
Society, Versus Arthritis), professional organisations (British Society for Rheumatology, Royal College of General
Practitioners) and public bodies (NICE, HQIP):
1. Plain language summaries to accompany all publications, co-written with my patient advisory group;
2. Social media summaries;
3. Public engagement events, via NIHR, RCGP and BSR networks;
4. Research open-days and science festivals;
5. Newsletters.
Impact statement: The goal of my Fellowship is to improve patient care by maximising the uptake of guideline-
recommended care:
 My research will directly benefit patients with gout, through reductions in pain, disability and
complications (e.g. erosive joint damage and kidney disease).
 For NHS clinicians, the intervention I develop will reduce unnecessary workload and serve as a
framework for delivering best practice care for other diseases.
 For commissioners, better disease control will reduce the number of avoidable consultations,
hospitalisations and complications.

signals.signal_text: Use of co-design, participatory methods, or consultations

criteria.rationale: While the plan is robust, the specific mechanisms for ongoing feedback from the patient advisory group during the trial phase could be elaborated further, though the general framework is strong.

criteria.name: Inclusive Research Design
criteria.definition: Consideration of inclusivity throughout the research lifecycle, including participant diversity and accessibility.

signals.signal_text: Explicit strategies for diverse recruitment
Adaptive methods or accessibility considerations
Equity and participation indicators

criteria.pros: The application explicitly plans to stratify analysis by ethnicity, deprivation, and comorbidity to address healthcare inequality (secdrp__001_k). It includes purposive sampling of under-served groups for focus groups and considers accessibility in the intervention design (secdrp__001_n, secdrp__001_s).
criteria.drawbacks: While the analysis plan is inclusive, specific adaptive methods for ensuring participation of hard-to-reach groups during the trial recruitment phase could be further detailed, though the focus group strategy is strong.
criteria.rationale: While the analysis plan is inclusive, specific adaptive methods for ensuring participation of hard-to-reach groups during the trial recruitment phase could be further detailed, though the focus group strategy is strong.

evidence.text:
ovides NICE-recommended care for people with gout. I will use a
intervention,34,35
person-based approach to develop this following the principles outlined in the MRC/NIHR
complex intervention development framework.36 The intervention will be deliverable as part of routine care by
primary care clinicians, where the vast majority of gout is managed.
Methods
Stakeholders: I will establish a core stakeholder group, who I will consult throughout the stages listed below,
including people with gout, healthcare professionals, commissioners and third-sector representatives.
Focus groups: Three focus groups will be convened to encourage open discussion among those with shared
experiences and develop a shared understanding of the challenges of achieving optimal gout care:
 Focus group one will include people with gout, carers and relatives. I will use the links I have formed with
patient organisations (including the UK Gout Society) to purposively sample patients from diverse
backgrounds, including ethnic minority and under-served communities, older patients, and those with
multiple long-term conditions.
 Focus group two will include healthcare professionals from a broad range of localities

bled, cluster-randomised trial in a primary care setting, to evaluate the
implementation and effectiveness of the intervention from Workstream 2. All study data will be automatically
captured in OpenSAFELY and analysed using the code I develop in Workstream 1. No manual data collection will
be required.
This methodology will be readily adaptable for other interventions and diseases, and will be shared openly
including with my collaborators at NICE.
Methods
Study design: Two-arm, parallel-group, cluster-randomised trial, with general practices as the unit of
randomisation. I have selected this study design as the intervention will be delivered at practice-level, reducing
treatment contamination between individuals.
Target population: People aged ≥18 years, registered with general practices in England, who consult a primary
care clinician for gout. Every patient consulting for gout at participating practices will be eligible; there will be no
exclusion criteria.
Intervention: Participating practices will be randomised 1:1 to receive:
The intervention package from Workstream 2; or

 Usual care.
The intervention will be delivered by primary care clinicians as part of routine care. It

sub_criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
sub_criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides a highly detailed and structured training plan. It explicitly lists specific courses (MSc in Health Data Science, Developing and Evaluating Complex Interventions, Cluster Randomised Trials), placements (OpenSAFELY, NICE, University of Tsukuba), and leadership programs (NIHR Future-Focused Leadership). The plan is sequenced by year (Years 1-4) and directly links each training activity to the specific workstreams of the research proposal (e.g., MSc for Workstreams 1 & 3, Complex Interventions course for Workstream 2).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details were identified; the training plan is comprehensive, specific, and fully evidenced.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details were identified; the training plan is comprehensive, specific, and fully evidenced.

Risk mitigation
Data access: I have already been granted approval by NHS England to analyse gout management using
OpenSAFELY. OpenSAFELY’s remit now covers non-COVID-19-related projects,23 and I have requested
approval to cover all analyses, with the support of my collaborators at OpenSAFELY.
me.20
Privacy: No individual patient data will be visible or transferred to Analysis code will be developed using
dummy data. Only summary-level outputs will be released following strict disclosivity checks. All platform activity
will be publicly-logged for transparency.
Code development: From my pilot work, I have developed analysis code/codelists for many of the variables I
require, for which there are low levels of missingness.
Personal development
To further my skills in computational analysis of complex health datasets, I will complete a part-time MSc in Health
Data Science (LSHTM) and a training placement with the OpenSAFELY team (University of Oxford).
Workstream 2 (months 12-24): Developing an intervention
Aim
To develop a multi-faceted intervention that pr

nd
patients, using think-aloud interviews to generate a detailed understanding of user engagement.41 Potential
barriers to successful implementation will be identified, and intervention components modified to optimise their
utility and acceptability.
Risk mitigation
Development time: All digital intervention components will utilise functionality already available within primary care
software (SystmOne/EMIS/Ardens). This will greatly reduce development time and ensure familiarity amongst
clinicians. I have begun scoping work to develop these solutions (including NICE-aligned decision-support tools
with Ardens).
Stakeholder buy-in: I have built an extensive network of collaborators, including patients, primary/secondary care
clinicians, allied health professionals, and organisations including NICE, NHS England and Ardens, to maximise
input from a broad range of stakeholders.
Personal development
I will complete the Developing and Evaluating Complex Interventions course at the National Centre for Research
Methods, led by the authors of the MRC/NIHR Complex Intervention Development Framework.

Proposed training and development programme
Training in skills necessary to support the proposed research
My NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and Clinical Lectureship have provided me with a strong foundation in big data
analysis, epidemiology and implementation science. An Advanced Fellowship would give me the opportunity to
develop new skills that will be incredibly helpful for this research proposal and in my future career as an
independent researcher in data-driven quality improvement:
 In Years 1 and 2, I will complete a part-time MSc in Health Data Science at the London School of
Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. This course aligns closely with the skills I will be using in Workstreams 1
and 3 of my proposal. It will be a fantastic opportunity to advance my skills as a health data scientist,
including modules dedicated to machine learning, predictive modelling and Bayesian analysis.
 In Year 2, I will complete the Developing and Evaluating Complex Interventions course at the
National Centre for Research Methods, University of Southampton. This course is led by Dr Kathyrn
Skivington, lead author of the MRC/NIHR Complex Intervention Development Framework, which I will be
using to develo
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
p a complex intervention in Workstream 2 of my proposal. The skills I learn in this course
will complement the training I received in Health Behaviour Change (University of Oxford) and
Implementation Science (King’s College London) during my Doctoral Fellowship.
 In Year 3, I will complete the Design and Analysis of Cluster Randomised and Stepped Wedge Trials
course at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. This course is led by Professor John
Bradley, an expert in cluster randomised trials. It covers many of the skills I will be utilising in Workstream
3 of my proposal, including cluster randomisation procedures, participant- and cluster-level analysis of
treatment effects, and ethical considerations in cluster randomised trials.
These courses will complement the training and expertise available to me at King’s College London, including
from experts in epidemiology (Professor James Galloway), data-enabled trial design (Professor Martin Gulliford),
experimental medicine (Professor Andrew Cope), statistics (Professor Sam Norton), implementation science
(Centre for Implementation Science), and PPIE (Dr Nicky Wilson).
Placements with other groups
During Year 1 of my Fell

owship, I will complete a 3-month placement (1 day a week) with software developers
at the Bennett Institute for Applied Data Science, University of Oxford. This will give me the opportunity to
learn one-on-one from the team of professional coders, database engineers and data visualisation experts behind
the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment (led by Professor Ben Goldacre), which I will be utilising in
Workstreams 1 and 3.
Throughout my Fellowship, I will be working closely with the Data Analytics and Implementation Support teams
at NICE to develop the infrastructure needed to monitor the real-world uptake of clinical guidelines. This will be an
incredible learning and career development opportunity. It will enable me to influence the development and
implementation of national guidelines across numerous disease areas.

sub_criteria.name: Suitability to Personal Development and Project Needs
sub_criteria.definition: How well the proposed training addresses the applicant’s current skills gaps and supports successful project delivery.

signals.signal_text: Identification of personal skill gaps
Clear mapping between training activities and project tasks
Evidence that proposed training is targeted rather than generic
Mention of mentorship, workshops, or formal courses relevant to development needs

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant clearly identifies skill gaps relative to their current foundation (e.g., needing advanced skills in machine learning, Bayesian analysis, and complex intervention design) and maps specific training activities to fill these gaps. The training is highly targeted, with courses led by experts in the specific methodologies required (e.g., Professor John Bradley for cluster trials, Dr Kathryn Skivington for complex interventions). Mentorship is explicitly integrated, with bi-monthly meetings with supervisors and a project steering group.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details were identified; the alignment between training and project needs is explicit and robust.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details were identified; the alignment between training and project needs is explicit and robust.

sub_criteria.name: Support for Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: The extent to which the training plan, and any professional practice components, contribute to the applicant’s long-term career goals.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of career goals
Connections between training activities and future career trajectory
Indications of skill transferability beyond the current project
Reference to professional practice or experiential learning where applicable

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly states career goals: to become an independent researcher in data-driven quality improvement and to secure tenure via the King's Fellow-to-Faculty scheme. The training plan directly supports this trajectory through leadership development (NIHR Future-Focused Leadership), international exposure (University of Tsukuba), and skill transferability (MSc, complex intervention courses). The plan includes professional practice components (clinical sessions, establishing a new clinic) that reinforce the practitioner-academic career path.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details were identified; the connection between training and long-term career aspirations is clear and well-supported.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details were identified; the connection between training and long-term career aspirations is clear and well-supported.

University of Tsukuba, Japan, to learn from clinical epidemiologists and quality improvement experts in a different
healthcare system.
6. Dissemination, outputs and impact
Healthcare outputs: My analysis code and methodology will be shared openly, including with my collaborators at
NICE. They will serve as a transparent, adaptable framework through which:
1. Care quality can be monitored in near real-time across multiple disease areas;
2. Patient impact can be quantified - particularly for under-served groups, for whom data gaps exist
currently;
3. Strategies to deliver best practice care can be evaluated efficiently.
I will be working directly with NICE to focus implementation-support activities towards reducing healthcare
inequality.
Academic outputs: I will publish my findings from each workstream in high-impact, peer-reviewed journals with
wide audiences. I will present my findings at national and international conferences, including to primary care and
quality improvement audiences.
Patient outputs: By adopting a data-driven, implementation science approach, it will maximise the likelihood of my
outputs enterin

valuate care quality in
near real-time. This is essential to improving patient outcomes and to organisations including NICE. Mark’s unique
skills in big data analysis and quality improvement methodology, combined with his clinical expertise, make him an
ideal person to lead this work.
Why King’s is the ideal host institution for Mark
The Centre for Rheumatic Diseases is a EULAR Centre of Research Excellence for rheumatology research. It has
been a launchpad for success for multiple clinical academics in the field of rheumatology and health services
research. Underlying the success of our department is cross-disciplinary leadership that bridges epidemiology,
clinical trials and qualitative methodology, integrated within NHS clinical practice. We have extremely supportive
team members, and an ethos of mentoring post-doctoral researchers through the next stage of their careers. We
prioritise training and career development over the quantity of research outputs, and we believe in the importance
of building a future generation of collaborative researchers who put patients at the centre of science.
Mark will benefit from our extensive experience of delivering high-quality health services
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
research using routinely-
collected healthcare data. He will receive training and methodological support from experts in clinical epidemiology
(Professor James Galloway), statistical methodology (Professor Sam Norton), experimental medicine (Professor
Andrew Cope), health psychology (Dr Joanna Hudson), and patient and public involvement (Dr Nicky Wilson). He
will have a wealth of expertise and training opportunities available in complex intervention development via the
Centre for Implementation Science at King’s, as well the support of a leading expert in data-enabled clinical trials
(Professor Martin Gulliford).
Mark will have access to a broad range of training in research methods, career development and mentorship
opportunities at King’s. Recognising that diversity of experience is essential at this stage of training, we actively
encourage training placements with other institutions and industry. Our department’s strong links with
organisations including NHS England, the Healthcare Quality Improvement Partnership, and the British Society for
Rheumatology, will provide Mark with the opportunity to become a leading collaborator in data-driven quality
improvement.
We have a strong

supported Mark in obtaining funding to bridge his transition from Doctoral Fellowship to Clinical Lecturer, and as a
co-applicant on NIHR HSDR and MRC programme grants. We are committed to supporting Mark during his
Advanced Fellowship, including through the provision of PhD students and research support. King’s also has a
Fellow-to-Faculty process which he would be eligible to pursue.
We are dedicated to ensuring equality of opportunity, recognising and celebrating our diversity and inclusion. We
have been a member of the Athena SWAN Gender Equality Charter since 2007, and King’s gained its most recent

(LGBTQIA+ equality). We are holders of the European Com

Project steering group: Annual meetings to provide strategic oversight and ensure successful delivery of my

Name Professor James Galloway
Institution King's College London
Position Professor of Rheumatology
Description of support provided
James Galloway is a Professor of Rheumatology at King’s College London and Honorary Consultant
Rheumatologist at King’s College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust. He is the lead methodologist for the National
Early Inflammatory Arthritis Audit in England and Wales; methodology advisor to the British Society for
Rheumatology; and Deputy Director of the King’s College London Clinical Research Facility. Professor Galloway’s
expertise in epidemiology, quality improvement and clinical trials, make him ideally placed to support my research
proposal and career development.
Professor Galloway has been a fantastic mentor to me throughout my clini
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
cal academic career. He was my
primary supervisor during my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, and is the academic supervisor for my NIHR Clinical
Lectureship. He has been incredibly supportive of me in developing my own research niche in data-driven quality
improvement, and in offering me his time and expertise as I developed this Advanced Fellowship proposal.
Professor Galloway is ideally placed to support my development into an independent health researcher. He has
extensive experience as a supervisor and early career mentor, and he is Programme Director for Integrated
Academic Training at King’s College London. He has several senior management roles within higher education,
and collaborates closely with national organisations including NHS England, CQC and HQIP, to whom my
research is closely aligned. Importantly, Professor Galloway is familiar with the challenges of navigating clinical
work as an NHS rheumatologist, alongside a successful academic career.

Name Associate Professor Laura Coates
Institution University of Oxford
Position NIHR Clinician Scientist
Description of support provided
Laura Coates is an associate professor and previous NIHR Clinicia

evidence.text:
n Scientist at the University of Oxford, and
honorary consultant rheumatologist at Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust. She is based outside of
my institution and will be less directly involved in the day-to-day running of my project, with a greater focus on my
career development and progression.
Professor Coates is a fantastic role model for how to build a successful career as a health researcher. She has
extensive experience of delivering successful grants, including a £1.8 million NIHR EME grant exploring precision
medicine in psoriatic arthritis, and a £2.3 million NIHR HSDR grant evaluating whether patient-initiated care leads
to improved outcomes in rheumatology. She is a co-lead on international consortia grants totalling over €30
million. She has a strong background in clinical trials and translational medicine, and has led several national and
international clinical guidelines. She is a leading clinical academic in the management of psoriatic arthritis – an
inflammatory joint condition that, like gout, is associated with multiple long-term health conditions (including
metabolic syndrome) and adverse health outcomes if undertreated. During her NIHR Fellowship,

she established
an NHS service that delivers optimal (‘treat-to-target’) management for patients with psoriatic arthritis; something I
hope to replicate for patients with gout during my Fellowship.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 3
Name Professor Edward Roddy
Institution Keele University
Position Professor of Rheumatology
Description of support provided
Edward Roddy is a Professor of Rheumatology at Keele University and Honorary Consultant Rheumatologist at
the Haywood Hospital, Midlands Partnership University NHS Foundation Trust. He is Chair of the British Society
for Rheumatology (BSR) Guidelines Steering Group.
Professor Roddy is a leading expert in the management of gout. He has led numerous, highly successful, research
grants in this field, spanning epidemiology and clinical trials, including grants funded by the NIHR Health
Technology Assessment, NIHR Research for Patient Benefit Programme, and NIHR School for Primary Care
Research. He led the 2017 BSR Gout Guideline and was clinical topic advisor to the 2022 NICE Gout Guideline.
He therefore has critical insight into the guideline I will be using to develop a data-driven approach to monitoring
guideline uptake.

evidence.text:
and analyse factors associated with sub-optimal care. I will demonstrate
how a data-enabled trial can evaluate quality improvement interventions efficiently using entirely routinely-
collected data. My proposal aligns closely with NIHR’s vision to harness data-enabled trials,5 and NICE’s strategy
for data-driven guideline evaluation.6
To achieve these aims, I will use the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment. Through OpenSAFELY,
we can now analyse electronic health data in near real-time for 58 million people, covering >99% of England’s
population. Privacy safeguards are greater than for any other data platform: no individual patient data are visible
to researchers, and all analysis code and platform activity are publicly logged.
To develop this methodology, I have chosen gout as an exemplar disease with a persistent evidence-
practice gap. Gout is a common inflammatory disease affecting 1 in 40 adults, characterised by severe joint pain,
disability and early mortality.7 It is a hallmark of the metabolic syndrome, with patients often experiencing multiple
long-term conditions, including chronic kidney and cardiovascular disease.8,9 There are highly-effective, urate-
lowering th

nvironment during the COVID-19 pandemic has
transformed our ability to deliver health data analyses for the whole of England:
 There is data coverage for >99% of England’s population;20
 Primary care data are updated weekly, and secondary care data monthly;20
 There is linkage to numerous additional datasets, including high-cost drugs and referral-to-treatment
times;20
 Privacy safeguards are greater than for any other platform.20,53
In my pilot work, I showed that OpenSAFELY can be used to evaluate the impact of the pandemic on long-
term conditions.1,2 I demonstrated that inflammatory arthritis diagnoses fell by 40% during the early pandemic,
while highlighting the remarkable adaptation of the NHS in the face of unprecedented pressures.1,2 These
analyses required no manual data collection; the number of captured diagnoses was 5-times greater than the
existing (manually-collected) National Audit programme;22 and these analyses can be updated regularly using
electronic dashboards linked to OpenSAFELY.
NHS England recently expanded OpenSAFELY’s use beyond COVID-19-related projects,23 making this
proposal timely and relevant. I will use this incredible resource to develop a data-

evidence.text:
valuate care quality in
near real-time. This is essential to improving patient outcomes and to organisations including NICE. Mark’s unique
skills in big data analysis and quality improvement methodology, combined with his clinical expertise, make him an
ideal person to lead this work.
Why King’s is the ideal host institution for Mark
The Centre for Rheumatic Diseases is a EULAR Centre of Research Excellence for rheumatology research. It has
been a launchpad for success for multiple clinical academics in the field of rheumatology and health services
research. Underlying the success of our department is cross-disciplinary leadership that bridges epidemiology,
clinical trials and qualitative methodology, integrated within NHS clinical practice. We have extremely supportive
team members, and an ethos of mentoring post-doctoral researchers through the next stage of their careers. We
prioritise training and career development over the quantity of research outputs, and we believe in the importance
of building a future generation of collaborative researchers who put patients at the centre of science.
Mark will benefit from our extensive experience of delivering high-quality health services

(Professor James Galloway), statistical methodology (Professor Sam Norton), experimental medicine (Professor
Andrew Cope), health psychology (Dr Joanna Hudson), and patient and public involvement (Dr Nicky Wilson). He
will have a wealth of expertise and training opportunities available in complex intervention development via the
Centre for Implementation Science at King’s, as well the support of a leading expert in data-enabled clinical trials
(Professor Martin Gulliford).
Mark will have access to a broad range of training in research methods, career development and mentorship
opportunities at King’s. Recognising that diversity of experience is essential at this stage of training, we actively
encourage training placements with other institutions and industry. Our department’s strong links with
organisations including NHS England, the Healthcare Quality Improvement Partnership, and the British Society for
Rheumatology, will provide Mark with the opportunity to become a leading collaborator in data-driven quality
improvement.

Project steering group: Annual meetings to provide strategic oversight and ensure successful delivery of my

re for people with gout and other crystal

with very few dedicated clinics in the UK for people with severe gout (e.g. those with end-stage renal disease,
severe tophaceous gout or recurrent hospitalisations). It will be fantastic opportunity to establish and lead such a

RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1
Name Professor James Galloway
Institution King's College London
Position Professor of Rheumatology
Description of support provided
James Galloway is a Professor of Rheumatology at King’s College London and Honorary Consultant
Rheumatologist at King’s College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust. He is the lead methodologist for the National
Early Inflammatory Arthritis Audit in England and Wales; methodology advisor to the British Society for
Rheumatology; and Deputy Director of the King’s College London Clinical Research Facility. Professor Galloway’s
expertise in epidemiology, quality improvement and clinical trials, make him ideally placed to support my research
proposal and career development.
Professor Galloway has been a fantastic mentor to me throughout my clini
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
cal academic career. He was my
primary supervisor during my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, and is the academic supervisor for my NIHR Clinical
Lectureship. He has been incredibly supportive of me in developing my own research niche in data-driven quality
improvement, and in offering me his time and expertise as I developed this Advanced Fellowship proposal.
Professor Galloway is ideally placed to support my development into an independent health researcher. He has
extensive experience as a supervisor and early career mentor, and he is Programme Director for Integrated
Academic Training at King’s College London. He has several senior management roles within higher education,
and collaborates closely with national organisations including NHS England, CQC and HQIP, to whom my
research is closely aligned. Importantly, Professor Galloway is familiar with the challenges of navigating clinical
work as an NHS rheumatologist, alongside a successful academic career.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 2
Name Associate Professor Laura Coates
Institution University of Oxford
Position NIHR Clinician Scientist
Description of support provided
Laura Coates is an associate professor and previous NIHR Clinicia
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
n Scientist at the University of Oxford, and
honorary consultant rheumatologist at Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust. She is based outside of
my institution and will be less directly involved in the day-to-day running of my project, with a greater focus on my
career development and progression.
Professor Coates is a fantastic role model for how to build a successful career as a health researcher. She has
extensive experience of delivering successful grants, including a £1.8 million NIHR EME grant exploring precision
medicine in psoriatic arthritis, and a £2.3 million NIHR HSDR grant evaluating whether patient-initiated care leads
to improved outcomes in rheumatology. She is a co-lead on international consortia grants totalling over €30
million. She has a strong background in clinical trials and translational medicine, and has led several national and
international clinical guidelines. She is a leading clinical academic in the management of psoriatic arthritis – an
inflammatory joint condition that, like gout, is associated with multiple long-term health conditions (including
metabolic syndrome) and adverse health outcomes if undertreated. During her NIHR Fellowship,
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
she established
an NHS service that delivers optimal (‘treat-to-target’) management for patients with psoriatic arthritis; something I
hope to replicate for patients with gout during my Fellowship.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 3
Name Professor Edward Roddy
Institution Keele University
Position Professor of Rheumatology
Description of support provided
Edward Roddy is a Professor of Rheumatology at Keele University and Honorary Consultant Rheumatologist at
the Haywood Hospital, Midlands Partnership University NHS Foundation Trust. He is Chair of the British Society
for Rheumatology (BSR) Guidelines Steering Group.
Professor Roddy is a leading expert in the management of gout. He has led numerous, highly successful, research
grants in this field, spanning epidemiology and clinical trials, including grants funded by the NIHR Health
Technology Assessment, NIHR Research for Patient Benefit Programme, and NIHR School for Primary Care
Research. He led the 2017 BSR Gout Guideline and was clinical topic advisor to the 2022 NICE Gout Guideline.
He therefore has critical insight into the guideline I will be using to develop a data-driven approach to monitoring
guideline uptake.

evidence.text:
and analyse factors associated with sub-optimal care. I will demonstrate
how a data-enabled trial can evaluate quality improvement interventions efficiently using entirely routinely-
collected data. My proposal aligns closely with NIHR’s vision to harness data-enabled trials,5 and NICE’s strategy
for data-driven guideline evaluation.6
To achieve these aims, I will use the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment. Through OpenSAFELY,
we can now analyse electronic health data in near real-time for 58 million people, covering >99% of England’s
population. Privacy safeguards are greater than for any other data platform: no individual patient data are visible
to researchers, and all analysis code and platform activity are publicly logged.
To develop this methodology, I have chosen gout as an exemplar disease with a persistent evidence-
practice gap. Gout is a common inflammatory disease affecting 1 in 40 adults, characterised by severe joint pain,
disability and early mortality.7 It is a hallmark of the metabolic syndrome, with patients often experiencing multiple
long-term conditions, including chronic kidney and cardiovascular disease.8,9 There are highly-effective, urate-
lowering th

nvironment during the COVID-19 pandemic has
transformed our ability to deliver health data analyses for the whole of England:
 There is data coverage for >99% of England’s population;20
 Primary care data are updated weekly, and secondary care data monthly;20
 There is linkage to numerous additional datasets, including high-cost drugs and referral-to-treatment
times;20
 Privacy safeguards are greater than for any other platform.20,53
In my pilot work, I showed that OpenSAFELY can be used to evaluate the impact of the pandemic on long-
term conditions.1,2 I demonstrated that inflammatory arthritis diagnoses fell by 40% during the early pandemic,
while highlighting the remarkable adaptation of the NHS in the face of unprecedented pressures.1,2 These
analyses required no manual data collection; the number of captured diagnoses was 5-times greater than the
existing (manually-collected) National Audit programme;22 and these analyses can be updated regularly using
electronic dashboards linked to OpenSAFELY.
NHS England recently expanded OpenSAFELY’s use beyond COVID-19-related projects,23 making this
proposal timely and relevant. I will use this incredible resource to develop a data-

evidence.text:
actice Award by the British Society for Rheumatology, and has been implemented at three other hospitals
nationally (Guy’s and St Thomas’ Hospital, Princess Royal University Hospital, Stockport NHS Foundation Trust).
During my Doctoral Fellowship, I was given the opportunity to lead the evidence review for a national guideline on
prescribing immunomodulatory drugs in pregnancy, commissioned by the British Society for Rheumatology. I
synthesised evidence from over 27,000 articles, managed a team of 4 evidence reviewers, and provided
methodological oversight. I liaised with a working group of 24 multi-disciplinary experts and patient
representatives. I published the guideline as first author (most-cited manuscript in Rheumatology,
2023: https://doi.org/10.1093/rheumatology/keac551), and I am regularly in correspondence about the implications
of the guideline. I was invited by the British Society for Rheumatology to support rheumatology trainees in taking
on guideline roles, and I have been invited to join the working group of another national guideline.
Patient and public engagement:
Patient involvement has been fundamental to the success of my doctoral and post-doctoral research - th

e goal of
which is to improve the quality of care for people with arthritis and other long-term conditions. I work closely with
patient organisations (including as a Trustee of the UK Gout Society) to ensure my research benefits patients and
the wider public. I have been interviewed about my research on BBC Radio 4 (Inside Health – James Gallagher),
recorded podcasts for the British Society for Rheumatology, Facebook Live events for the National Axial
Spondyloarthritis Society, and speaker events for Versus Arthritis. The experiences of patients – gathered through
interviews, stakeholder workshops, PPIE events and direct clinical care – have shaped all aspects of this
application.
Other skills:
I have been trained in epidemiology and statistical methodology (London School of Hygiene and Tropical
Medicine; University of Aberdeen; NIHR BRC), implementation science (University of Oxford; KCL), and the safe
use of health data (Office for National Statistics). I am actively involved in the NIHR Clinical Trials Facility at King’s
College Hospital, and have been a co-investigator on 8 studies (including multi-centre RCTs). I was awarded the
British Society for Rheumatology Young Investiga

plan or the treatment that would usually be given. All study data will be collected automatically in
OpenSAFELY, with no extra data needed from patients or doctors. The following results will be compared
for patients treated for gout over a 12-month period:
Whether patients received guideline-recommended treatments (e.g. allopurinol);
o
If they developed complications of gout (e.g. kidney problems);

o How often they visited their GP or hospital.
How will the results be used?
My research will directly benefit people with gout, by reducing pain and disability. It will help doctors and nurses
give better care to their patients. It will reduce costs for the NHS and help focus resources on areas where
improvement is needed most. I will be working closely with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
(NICE) to make sure my research benefits people with other long-term conditions. I will share all of my findings
openly through conferences, research papers, and by working with our professional and patient communities.

events, a

nd discussions with patient organisations. Throughout the project, I will be working together with patient

evidence.text:
methodology,
I have chosen gout as an exemplar disease with a persistent evidence-practice gap.
I will complete three workstreams:
1. Develop a reproducible analytic framework to monitor the implementation of NICE guidance;
2. Design a multi-faceted intervention to maximise uptake of guideline-recommended care;
3. Evaluate this intervention using a data-enabled, cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY.
Methods (timelines for delivery)
Workstream 1 (months 0-12): Using anonymised GP and hospital data for 58 million people in OpenSAFELY, I will
systematically map recommendations from the NICE Gout Guideline and analyse temporal trends in their
implementation. I will use regression-modelling approaches to identify predictors of sub-optimal care, and evaluate
the impact on outcomes including comorbidities and hospitalisations. Data-driven dashboards will feedback
performance against NICE standards at national, regional and local levels.
Workstream 2 (months 12-24): I will convene focus groups with broad stakeholder representation to identify
barriers and facilitators of optimal gout care. I will collaborate with implementation science experts at King's
College London to develop an evid

How will the results be used?
My research will directly benefit people with gout, by reducing pain and disability. It will help doctors and nurses
give better care to their patients. It will reduce costs for the NHS and help focus resources on areas where
improvement is needed most. I will be working closely with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
(NICE) to make sure my research benefits people with other long-term conditions. I will share all of my findings
openly through conferences, research papers, and by working with our professional and patient communities.
Patient and public involvement (PPI)
My research plan has been shaped by the experiences of patients, gathered through interviews, patient and carer
events, a

evidence.text:
ence-based intervention package that provides NICE-recommended care for
people with gout, incorporating digital solutions within electronic health record software and educational content.
Workstream 3 (months 24-60): I will conduct a data-enabled, cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY to evaluate
the intervention from Workstream 2. General practices (n=38) will be randomised 1:1 to receive this intervention or
usual care. All study data will be automatically captured in OpenSAFELY and analysed using the framework
developed in Workstream 1. Outcomes will include:
 The uptake of NICE-recommended gout treatments (e.g. allopurinol);
 The incidence of gout-associated comorbidities (e.g. kidney disease);
 Consultation/hospitalisation rates.
Anticipated impact
My research will directly benefit patients with gout, by reducing pain and disability. Better disease control will
prevent avoidable hospitalisations and complications. For policy makers, it will help target strategies towards
reducing healthcare inequality. I will be collaborating with NICE throughout my Fellowship to ensure this
methodology is readily adaptable for other guidelines and diseases. I will share all of my analysis

code openly, and
communicate my findings through conferences, research papers, social media, and PPIE events.

g our health system and benefitting patients. I will use multiple approaches to inform study
participants, patients and the wider population, utilising collaborations with patient organisations (UK Gout
Society, Versus Arthritis), professional organisations (British Society for Rheumatology, Royal College of General
Practitioners) and public bodies (NICE, HQIP):
1. Plain language summaries to accompany all publications, co-written with my patient advisory group;
2. Social media summaries;
3. Public engagement events, via NIHR, RCGP and BSR networks;
4. Research open-days and science festivals;
5. Newsletters.
Impact statement: The goal of my Fellowship is to improve patient care by maximising the uptake of guideline-
recommended care:
 My research will directly benefit patients with gout, through reductions in pain, disability and
complications (e.g. erosive joint damage and kidney disease).
 For NHS clinicians, the intervention I develop will reduce unnecessary workload and serve as a
framework for delivering best practice care for other diseases.
 For commissioners, better disease control will reduce the number of avoidable consultations,
hospitalisations and complications.

linical Audit programmes in England and Wales using big data (Russell

HSDR grant (£2.3 million), led by Professor Laura Coates (University of Oxford), in which I am evaluating the real-
world effectiveness of the NHS England Patient-Initiated Follow-Up programme using electronic health record data
(OpenSAFELY; Hospital Episode Statistics). This project includes a pragmatic, multi-centre RCT (30 sites), which
provides me with an excellent grounding in the skillsets I will be using in this Advanced Fellowship.
In January 2024, I was awarded a competitive, industry-funded grant to fund 6 months of post-doctoral research. I
used National Clinical Audit data to demonstrate that under-served groups (particularly ethnic minorities and older
adults) are much less likely to receive biologic medications for rheumatoid arthritis than other patients (under peer-
review, Lancet Rheumatology).

I thoroughly enjoy teaching, and regularly deliver clinically-focused seminars and lectures on Quality Improvement

Methods

Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
To generate ideas for my Fellowship, I spoke with 10 people who had recently been treated for gout, using
semi-structured interviews to capture a diverse range of perspectives and priorities for improvement. The people I
spoke with highlighted several barriers to better gout care. This included a lack of information on the role of
preventative treatments; infrequent blood tests to monitor their urate levels; poor coordination of care; and a
stigma that gout is a self-inflicted disease due to bad diet. People from under-served groups, particularly those
with multiple long-term conditions, felt that their gout was viewed as unimportant, despite having a large impact on
their quality of life. These are issues that I have encountered repeatedly for people with gout during my clinical
career, despite numerous studies showing that the symptoms and complications of gout can be prevented
effectively with guideline-recommended treatments such as allopurinol.
To develop my proposal further, I convened a stakeholder meeting and a patient and public engagement
event, and collaborate

any training and support provided
My public partners will be an integral part of my Fellowship. They will offer advice as members of my steering
group, and will be invited to join our research team. The Centre for Rheumatic Diseases has an established
process for arranging honorary research contracts and for supporting patient advisory groups. All patient
involvement will be supported by appropriate reimbursement, budgeted for in this proposal.
With the support of the UK Gout Society, I will invite additional public partners to form an advisory group, with
broad representation from under-served groups, lay members, patient organisations (including Dr Benjamin Ellis,
policy advisor to Versus Arthritis), and a patient and public involvement (PPI) coordinator from King’s. The group
will oversee PPI strategy, including ethics approval documentation, co-development of educational materials and
focus group topic guides, interpreting study results, dissemination planning, and a patient-led presentation at the
British Society for Rheumatology (BSR) conference.

For quality assurers (e.g. CQC, HQIP), it will provide the information needed to highlight unwarranted

7. Project Management
Project steering group: Annual meetings to provide strategic oversight and ensure successful delivery of my
project aims. Members will include my patient partners, mentors, Shaun Rowark (NICE representative), Professor
Abhishek (gout/trial expertise), Dr Chowla (GP), Dr Hudson (qualitative methodologist), Professor Norton
(statistician), and Dr Ellis (policy advisor to Versus Arthritis).
Patient advisory group: Meetings at the beginning of each workstream, then annually, to oversee PPIE strategy,
including co-developing educational materials, interpreting study results, and facilitating dissemination. Members
will include patients, lay members, patient organisation representatives, and a PPIE coordinator from KCL.

d with the leading patient organisation for people living with gout in the UK (the UK Gout
Society). The feedback received was incredibly informative: whereas my previous research had focused on
improving gout care in a hospital setting, it became clear that many of the difficulties patients continue to
encounter are in the community (where most gout care is provided). Patients reiterated that a single, focused
intervention would not address the many barriers to optimal gout care. Instead, a multi-faceted intervention,
incorporating both patient-centred education and clinician-facing solutions (e.g. digital solutions that streamline
care) would be essential. This has informed the focus and framework for my study plan.
Two public partners – Joel and Erman – who are experts by experience, have agreed to support me throughout
my Fellowship. They have extensive experience of the issues my research seeks to address, and their input has
been hugely beneficial in shaping this proposal, from study design to intervention ideas and dissemination plans.

criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

criteria.pros: The application provides robust evidence of integrating lived experience. It details co-design activities including patient interviews and stakeholder meetings that explicitly shifted the research focus to community care (secppi__001_a, secppi__001_b). A patient advisory group is established to co-develop topic guides and interpret results (secdrp__001_n, secppi__001_c). The Plain English Summary and Scientific Abstract confirm that patient partners shaped the study design and dissemination plans (secpesor__001_c, secsa__001_b).
criteria.drawbacks: While the involvement is extensive, the specific 'changes made to study design' (wp.1.b) are described generally (e.g., shifting focus) rather than listing specific methodological alterations resulting from patient feedback, though the evidence is still strong.
criteria.rationale: While the involvement is extensive, the specific 'changes made to study design' (wp.1.b) are described generally (e.g., shifting focus) rather than listing specific methodological alterations resulting from patient feedback, though the evidence is still strong.

evidence.text:
actice Award by the British Society for Rheumatology, and has been implemented at three other hospitals
nationally (Guy’s and St Thomas’ Hospital, Princess Royal University Hospital, Stockport NHS Foundation Trust).
During my Doctoral Fellowship, I was given the opportunity to lead the evidence review for a national guideline on
prescribing immunomodulatory drugs in pregnancy, commissioned by the British Society for Rheumatology. I
synthesised evidence from over 27,000 articles, managed a team of 4 evidence reviewers, and provided
methodological oversight. I liaised with a working group of 24 multi-disciplinary experts and patient
representatives. I published the guideline as first author (most-cited manuscript in Rheumatology,
2023: https://doi.org/10.1093/rheumatology/keac551), and I am regularly in correspondence about the implications
of the guideline. I was invited by the British Society for Rheumatology to support rheumatology trainees in taking
on guideline roles, and I have been invited to join the working group of another national guideline.
Patient and public engagement:
Patient involvement has been fundamental to the success of my doctoral and post-doctoral research - th

patient organisations (including as a Trustee of the UK Gout Society) to ensure my research benefits patients and
the wider public. I have been interviewed about my research on BBC Radio 4 (Inside Health – James Gallagher),
recorded podcasts for the British Society for Rheumatology, Facebook Live events for the National Axial
Spondyloarthritis Society, and speaker events for Versus Arthritis. The experiences of patients – gathered through
interviews, stakeholder workshops, PPIE events and direct clinical care – have shaped all aspects of this
application.
